rt_critics_consensus: For genre fans in the mood to watch some darkly funny mayhem,Bloody Helllives up to its title in all the best ways.
It's difficult to make a persuasive argument forThe Mummyas any kind of meaningful cinematic achievement, but it's undeniably fun to watch.
An initially intriguing sci-fi thriller that quickly veers into incoherence,Infiniteis as inane as it is inconsequential.
Director Gulager makes the most of what he's given; the resultingFeastoffers up some surprisingly tasty -- if far from nourishing -- morsels.
It's difficult to make a persuasive argument forThe Mummyas any kind of meaningful cinematic achievement, but it's undeniably fun to watch.
The Lazarus Effecthas a talented cast and the glimmer of an interesting idea, but wastes it all on insipid characters and dull, recycled plot points.
Above average slasher flick.
Stylish setting and masterfully choreographed fight scenes, but the story is low on credibility and high on excessive violence and gore.
A tough, highly stylized thriller with amazing sound design and car chases.
Frank Darabont's impressive camerawork and politically incisive script makeThe Mista truly frightening experience.

Controlis a work of art, thanks to its evocative black and white cinematography and sensational performances from Sam Riley and Samantha Morton. Even those not familiar with Joy Division can still appreciate the beauty of the film.
It's Only the End of the Worldis stocked with talent and boasts a story steeped in conflict, but the end result proves a disappointing misfire from writer-director Xavier Dolan.
Ambitious and well-intentioned,The Cutis visually arresting and at times, even emotionally piercing, but ultimately fails to transcend.
Neither intelligent enough to work as thought-provoking sci-fi nor trashy enough to provide B-movie thrills,The Last Days on Marsproves as cinematically barren as the titular planet.
Jaws 2never approaches the lingering thrills of its classic predecessor, but it's reasonably entertaining for a sequel that has no reason to exist.
While it's arguably more interesting on a visual level,Labyrinthprovides further proof of director Jim Henson's boundless imagination.
More tasteful than recent slasher flicks, butDead Silenceis undone by boring characters, bland dialogue, and an unnecessary and obvious twist ending.
The Cat O'Nine Tailsis a solidly entertaining Argento outing elevated by a well-chosen cast and the director's distinctive visual style.
Jeepers Creepers has a promising start. Unfortunately, the tension and suspense quickly deflates into genre cliches as movie goes on.
Man Finds Tapeis a smart, slow-burning directorial debut that delivers layered, imaginative horror with strong performances and compelling mysteries.
Shelby Oaksmay stumble in its final moments, but its atmospheric tension and emotional core firmly establish it as a chilling debut from director Chris Stuckmann.
Inept on almost every level, Alone in the Dark may not work as a thriller, but it's good for some head-slapping, incredulous laughter.
Elevated by David Fincher's directorial talent and Jodie Foster's performance,Panic Roomis a well-crafted, above-average thriller.
This well-intentioned documentary is too broad in its subjects to have any sort of cohesion.
Night Shiftbristles with pitch-perfect laughs thanks to Ron Howard's snappy direction and a side-splitting turn from Michael Keaton.
It's uneven and diverges from the source book, butWorld War Zstill brings smart, fast-moving thrills and a solid performance from Brad Pitt to the zombie genre.
Antiviralis well-crafted body horror, packed with interesting -- if not entirely subtle -- ideas.
Didn't Diemixes lo-fi visuals, humor, and horror effectively, but uneven pacing and a slight premise keep its drama from fully landing.
A muddled and campy MTV-styled vampire movie with lots of eye candy and bad accents.
Totally Killermay not take full advantage of its promising conceit, but this time-traveling horror/sci-fi mashup is still enjoyable overall.
Two Loversis a complex, intriguing, richly-acted romantic drama.
Thr3eis a shoddily made, thrill-free thriller that isn't half as good as the several movies it borrows from (Adaptation, Saw, Se7en).
If you only watch one art-world satire with horror overtones this year -- or most others -- it should probably beVelvet Buzzsaw.
Atmospheric horror built on layers of creeping dread,Honeydewfinds fresh frights in its familiar setup -- and portends great things for writer-director Devereux Milburn.
A beguiling odyssey that offsets its scrappy production values with sumptuously analog presentation,OBEXis a wildly inventive riff on the digital world.
My Bloody Valentinestumbles through a cinematic coal mine of mechanical scares, bland characters, and a contrived, blood-soaked plot that's as predictable as it is unimaginative.
Something in the Dirtreaffirms Justin Benson and Aaron Moorhead as sci-fi storytellers with a distinctive -- and darkly alluring -- vision.
For genre fans in the mood to watch some darkly funny mayhem,Bloody Helllives up to its title in all the best ways.
Although it sometimes struggles to make sense of its story,Dementerpresents a unique -- and chillingly dark -- perspective on healthcare horror.
Some will find the darkly funny, genre-bending incoherence ofJohn Dies at the Endcharming; some will feel its zany antics and gore lead to an unsatisfying payoff.
Cleverly written and directed, this is a tight, atmospheric and claustrophobic low budget British thriller.

A bona fide classic of midnight cinema, Tommy Wiseau's misguided masterpiece subverts the rules of filmmaking with a boundless enthusiasm that renders such mundanities as acting, screenwriting, and cinematography utterly irrelevant. You will never see a football the same way again.
Sweet, soulful, and smart, Spike Jonze'sHeruses its just-barely-sci-fi scenario to impart wryly funny wisdom about the state of modern human relationships.
This sequel is full of lavish costumes and elaborate sets, but lacks the heart and creativity of the originalElizabeth

Adding some surprising emotional layers onto the ghoulish bones ofFinal Destination's mythology,Bloodlinesingeniously executes grisly set pieces with precision and turns impending doom into outrageous fun.
Given the amount of talent assembled on both sides of the camera,Are You Herefalls bewilderingly flat.
Anaconda's pulpy pleasures are constricted by its own absurdity, but creature feature fans may enjoy its brazen silliness.
Uniting Jack Lemmon and a never-better Walter Matthau on the screen for the first time,The Fortune Cookiecontinues director Billy Wilder's winning streak of sweet comedies with a nice snap.
Bless the Childsquanders its talented cast on a plot that's more likely to inspire unintentional laughs than shivers.
Although it deserves credit for its strong cast and inclusive premise,They/Themis too tonally messy to cut more than skin deep.
With a sturdy framework but too many unnecessary parts,The Gatewayleads to a bit of a B-movie dead end.
A joyless excursion into the water that doesn't even produce good gore or nudity thanks to the neutered PG-13 rating.
This compassionate work of social criticism is also luminous, due to both its lyrical imagery and cast.
Dull and disappointing, the best that can be said forThe Amityville Horroris that it set a low bar for its many sequels and remakes.
Kicking Bloodovercomes evident budget limitations with solid acting and a vampire story that has an emotionally impactful twist.
A clone of THX 1183, Coma, and Logan's Run, The Island is another loud and bombastic Michael Bay movie where explosions and chases matter more than characters, dialogue, or plot.
Lean and solidly crafted,The Shallowstranscends tired shark-attack tropes with nasty thrills and a powerful performance from Blake Lively.

Though the genre is well worn at this point, director Jim Mickle focuses on strong characterization and eerie atmosphere to craft an effective apocalyptic vampire chiller that also manages to pack a mean punch.
In this creepy story of a man and his rodents, Glover seems born to play the oddball title character.
Let the Right One Inreinvigorates the seemingly tired vampire genre by effectively mixing scares with intelligent storytelling.
Police procedural meets werewolf flick inWolfen, a creepy creature feature with a surprisingly profound side.
It's uneven, as anthologies often are, butCreepshowis colorful, frequently funny, and treats its inspirations with infectious reverence.
It may not be the most effective holiday horror entry, butThe Advent Calendarhas its share of nasty treats for genre fans.
Tense, well-acted, and at once atmospheric as well as brutally impactful,Backcountrymarks a memorably assured debut from writer-director Adam MacDonald.
Stingshould satisfy horror fans in the mood for a creature feature, even if the end results likely won't linger in the memory.
Yet another predictable variation on the hoary old haunted-house movie, Darkness is an illogical, portentous mess.
The rich colors, sweet songs, adorable mice and endearing (if suffering) heroine make Cinderella a nostalgically lovely charmer.
Cannes Jury Prize-winner Fish Tank is gritty British realism at its very best, with flawless performances from newcomer Kate Jarvis, and Michael Fassbender.
Sergio Castellitto's latest film has a heavy dose of melodrama and is populated with unlikable characters.
A Gallic farce that wildly veers between tones with a feminist undercurrent throughout,The Balconettesmarks a promising creative progression for director-and-star Noémie Merlant.
Immersive and powerfully acted,Bad Hairoffers an unflinching yet gently endearing depiction of poverty's impact on youthful self-actualization.
Bereft of a single kernel of fear or excitement, thisChildren of the Cornsuggests the franchise is irrevocably lost in a maize of sub-mediocrity.
Anchored by Richard Gere's egoless performance,Oh Canadais a thorny and sometimes muddled memory play that fits solidly into director Paul Schrader's self-reflective filmography.
A smart mockumentary that presents a gory, funny, and obviously affectionate skewering of the slasher genre.
Val Kilmer delivers a powerhouse performance as one of rock's most incendiary figures, but unfortunately, Oliver Stone is unable to shed much light on the circus surrounding the star.
Night Shiftbristles with pitch-perfect laughs thanks to Ron Howard's snappy direction and a side-splitting turn from Michael Keaton.
Mothering Sundayworks at a frustratingly chilly remove, but involving performances and solid overall craft mean it's rarely less than engaging.
Ingmar Bergman makes a successful foray into horror withHour of the Wolf, infusing the demons that spring from creativity with his trademark psychological curiosity.
Spurts of dark humor gush out ofI Blame Society's satirical approach to filmmaking and celebrity culture, prodded by sharp societal observations from writer-director-star Gillian Wallace Horvat.
Powered by Chris Cooper's masterful performance,Breachis a tense and engaging portrayal of the FBI's infamous turncoat.
A killjoy entry into the debauched children's story horror canon,The Mean Onedelivers a seasonally macabre bag of lime green coal.
A darkly delirious satire rooted in real-life horror,El Condefinds Pablo Larraín revisiting familiar themes without losing their provocative power.
Part creature feature, part war movie, and part social commentary,Shadow in the Clouddoesn't always blend its ingredients evenly -- but it's frequently pulpy fun.
Its journey is never quite as revelatory as it could be, butThe Hitcherstands as a white-knuckle vision of horror, bolstered by Rutger Hauer's menacing performance.
It'll be too disturbing -- and meandering -- for some, butDogtoothis as disturbing and startlingly original as modern filmmaking gets.
Carla Gugino carriesGerald's Game's small-scale suspense with a career-defining performance.
This compassionate work of social criticism is also luminous, due to both its lyrical imagery and cast.
It may not be the killer thrill ride you'd expect from an action movie with a cast of this caliber, butRedstill thoroughly outshines most of its big-budget counterparts with its wit and style.
Disappointing resolution aside,Buguses its claustrophobic setting and cinéma vérité camerawork to tense, impressive effect.
Carried by its charismatic young cast,Better Watch Outis an adorably sinister holiday horror film.
Relying more on atmosphere than gore, Session 9 is effectively creepy.
The Sentinelstarts off well enough but quickly wears thin with too many plot holes and conventional action sequences.
The plot is been-there done-that generic, and none of the shock effects can do anything to build up suspense.
Fleeting moments of brainless monster mayhem aside,The Relicproves to be too predictably derivative for its own good.
Some interesting visuals, but the movie is as confusing as a dream.
Hereditaryuses its classic setup as the framework for a harrowing, uncommonly unsettling horror film whose cold touch lingers long beyond the closing credits.
A well-mounted production is undermined by a muddled, absurd storyline of questionable taste.
Bloody and brutal,Hunter Hunteris a hard-hitting survivalist thriller that ratchets its tension with precision.
Gosling commands the screen with a raw, electrifying performance.
After Life is an offbeat and tender exploration of memory, love, and life after death.
Mimicfinds director Guillermo del Toro struggling to inject his unique sensibilities into a studio picture - and delivering just enough genre thrills to recommend.
Boosted by Sam Rockwell's intense performance,Moonis a compelling work of science-fiction, and a promising debut from director Duncan Jones.
Bad Samaritandelivers a handful of genre thrills -- and a stellar central performance by David Tennant -- that help offset iffy direction and a disappointingly mundane story.
Working with admittedly familiar ingredients,Abominableoffers audiences a beautifully animated and overall engaging adventure that the whole family can enjoy.
Despite quirky and original writing, the subject matter feels too removed to produce laughs.
If you're interested in watching a battle between cockneys and zombies, this is the movie for you.
The ending could use a little work but this is otherwise another sterling example of David Fincher's iron grip on atmosphere and storytelling.
An entertaining slasher with a gender-bending, body-swapping twist, this horror-comedy juggles genres withFreakyfun results.
Powered by Holly Hunter's main performance,The Pianois a truth-seeking romance played in the key of erotic passion.
Malumlures you to clock in for another Mason-esqueLast Shiftat a haunted police station with sheer blunt lore and gore.
Featuring hammy performances and bland characters,Solois an all too straightforward actioner that's both predictable and instantly forgettable.
With a sturdy framework but too many unnecessary parts,The Gatewayleads to a bit of a B-movie dead end.
Intoxicating with its maxxximal style,MaXXXineis an uneven but vibrant pastiche that drives a stiletto through Hollywood's heart.

Despite lacking some of the book's subtler shadings, and suffering from some clumsy casting, Interview with a Vampire benefits from Neil Jordan's atmospheric direction and a surfeit of gothic thrills.
Numerous plot holes and poorly motivated characters preventNextfrom being the thought-provoking sci-fi flick it could've been.
Anaconda's pulpy pleasures are constricted by its own absurdity, but creature feature fans may enjoy its brazen silliness.
It might be more impressive on a technical level than as a piece of storytelling, butAvatarreaffirms James Cameron's singular gift for imaginative, absorbing filmmaking.
Even with the force of a "classic" behind it, remake fever can't hold up the hollowness of this style-drenchedOmen.
Bleak and darkly humorous, No Man's Land vividly illustrates the absurdity of war.
Taking the consequences of its predecessor seriously,Black Phone 2is a chilling sequel that mines memorable scares and thought-provoking themes from the scars that linger.
A derivative movie where the scares are few and things don't make much sense.
Hellbound: Hellraiser IIretains the twisted visual thrill of its predecessor, although seams in the plot are already starting to show.
Mimicfinds director Guillermo del Toro struggling to inject his unique sensibilities into a studio picture - and delivering just enough genre thrills to recommend.
Anchored by Sean Penn's powerhouse performance,Milkis a triumphant account of America's first openly gay man elected to public office.
See How They Runmay not do itself many favors by asking for Christie comparisons, but this is still one fun whodunit.
Director John Irvin wastes the efforts of a tremendously talented cast, making this oneGhost Storythat deserves to be drowned out by boos.
With its magical optical effects and enchanting performances, Jean Cocteau'sBeauty and the Beastremains the most surreal -- and soulful -- of the fairy tale's film adaptations.
Fleeting moments of brainless monster mayhem aside,The Relicproves to be too predictably derivative for its own good.
Friend Request's attempts to update old-school teen horror for the digital age do not, sadly, include memorable characters, fresh scares, or novel storytelling twists.
With little gore and a lot of creepy visuals,The Ringgets under your skin, thanks to director Gore Verbinski's haunting sense of atmosphere and an impassioned performance from Naomi Watts.
Shadow of the Vampireis frightening, compelling, and funny, and features an excellent performance by Willem Dafoe.
Unsaneunleashes Steven Soderbergh's inner B-movie maestro, wading into timeless psychological thriller territory and giving it a high-tech filmmaking spin.
Doctor Jekyllis a meandering reinterpretation whose gothic ambitions can't overcome its hollow storytelling and awkward tonal choices.
A fascinating, even-handed account of Hell Houses.
With its magical optical effects and enchanting performances, Jean Cocteau'sBeauty and the Beastremains the most surreal -- and soulful -- of the fairy tale's film adaptations.
An erotic thriller dulled by a messy narrative.
Run Sweetheart Run's heavy-handedness blunts the effectiveness of its message, but this remains a wily, unpredictable thriller.
If it fails to make the most of its intriguing premise,In the Mouth of Madnessremains a decent enough diversion for horror fans and John Carpenter completists.
Lords of Chaospresents a grimly compelling dramatization of a real-life music scene whose aggressively nihilistic aesthetic spilled over into fatal acts of violence.
Inept on almost every level, Alone in the Dark may not work as a thriller, but it's good for some head-slapping, incredulous laughter.
The movie stretches too long to be entertaining despite a strong cast.
Infamous for its shower scene, but immortal for its contribution to the horror genre. BecausePsychowas filmed with tact, grace, and art, Hitchcock didn't just create modern horror, he validated it.

Marking Alfred Hitchcock's return to England and first foray into viscerally explicit carnage,Frenzyfinds the master of horror regaining his grip on the audience's pulse -- and making their blood run cold.
A slow-burning horror story that takes spine-tingling advantage of its setting and cast,I Trapped the Devilmarks an auspicious debut for writer-director Josh Lobo.
The First Purgeshould satisfy fans of the franchise and filmgoers in the mood for violent vicarious thrills, even if its subtextual reach exceeds its grasp.
Beautiful scenery and a solid star turn from Anthony Mackie raiseElevationup to an extent, but this sci-fi thriller is too derivative to reach the peak of its potential.
Halloween 5: The Revenge Of Michael Myersfinds the series embracing crude slasher conventions with pedestrian scares, disposable characters, and aimless plotting.

Effectively playing on fundamental fears,Lovely, Dark, and Deepis a creepy calling card for debuting director Teresa Sutherland that's further distinguished by Georgina Campbell's haunting performance.
Devilishly examining the intersection of fame and obsession to thrilling effect, Alex Russell's directorial debut delivers on all fronts.
This satire on the art world is at times both clever and shallow, but its top-notch cast generates plenty of goodwill.
Thanks largely to Rebecca De Mornay's delightfully villainous turn in the central role,The Hand That Rocks the Cradleis an entertaining -- albeit rather silly -- domestic thriller.
A thrillingly effective update on a classic story,Depravedjolts a familiar monster back to life with a potent blend of timely themes and old-school chills.

Monotonously fast-paced to the point of exhaustion,Getawayoffers a reminder of the dangers in attempting to speed past coherent editing, character development, sensible dialogue, and an interesting plot.
Day Watchis frequently cheesy but it offers enough twists, surprises, and inventive action sequences to maintain viewer interest.
Berlin Syndromeoffers thriller fans an uncommonly well-written descent into dangerous obsession, enlivened by taut direction and a committed performance from Teresa Palmer.
A stylish and well-acted tonal blend,Medusaconfronts religious hypocrisy and sexism with invigorating anger.
George C. Scott's somber performance gives this haunted house horror a moving soul to go along with its harrowing scares.
It represents an occasionally uneven collision of genres, but all in all, Wladyslaw Pasikowski'sAftermathdelves into Holocaust guilt with power and efficiency.
The rare comedy thriller that's funny as well as thrilling,Get Duked!marks writer-director Ninian Doff as a filmmaker with a bright future.
Pitting actors against murderers in a self-aware struggle for stardom,Bagheadsuccessfully skates the borders of horror and comedy.
Val Kilmer delivers a powerhouse performance as one of rock's most incendiary figures, but unfortunately, Oliver Stone is unable to shed much light on the circus surrounding the star.
The Oak Roomcontains a taut, spooky tale that proves southern gothic can flourish in the snowy north.
They Remainrelies on rich atmosphere to effectively establish a sense of creeping dread that's almost enough to cover for its nagging narrative deficiencies.
The Abandonedspends so much time setting up the creepy atmosphere, that it forgets to inhabit it with genuine spooks or scares.
Jeepers Creepers has a promising start. Unfortunately, the tension and suspense quickly deflates into genre cliches as movie goes on.
The ending could use a little work but this is otherwise another sterling example of David Fincher's iron grip on atmosphere and storytelling.
Purportedly Vera Farmiga and Patrick Wilson's final outing as the Warrens,Last Ritesunderwhelms as a climactic chapter forThe Conjuringbut solidly upholds the franchise's spooky standards.
This satire on the art world is at times both clever and shallow, but its top-notch cast generates plenty of goodwill.
Love at First Bitecould use some more warm-blooded barbs to liven up its undead comedy, but George Hamilton's campy charisma gives the prince of darkness some welcome pizazz.
With a plot as creaky as the boat, Ghost Ship fails to deliver the scares.
All the atmospherics in Dark Water can't make up for the lack of genuine scares.
This uneven but amiable 1967 vampire picture is part horror spoof, part central European epic, and 100 percent Roman Polanski, whose signature sensibility colors every frame.
Spawnis an overbearing, over-violent film that adds little to the comic book adaptation genre.
Despite a promising start and a handful of solid scares,Night Swimis undone by a premise that just isn't strong enough to support a feature-length film.
Receiving some sparkle from Paul Rudd and Jenna Ortega's father-daughter rapport,Death of a Unicorn's broad satire is a bit too on the horn but makes for an entertainingly splattery creature feature.
An effective if knowingly silly Stephen King anthology that combines comedy and terror.
Though it is ultimately somewhat undone by its own lofty ambitions,The Devil's Advocateis a mostly effective blend of supernatural thrills and character exploration.
Krampusis gory good fun for fans of non-traditional holiday horror with a fondness for Joe Dante's B- movie classics, even if it doesn't have quite the savage bite its concept calls for.
This is where it all began for the Muscles from Brussels, but beyond Van Damme's athleticism,Bloodsportis a cliched, virtually plotless exercise in action movie recycling.
Though it boasts a talented cast, this thriller fails to make you care about the twisty mystery of the men's identities and situation.
While it struggles with pacing and an indulgently chaotic narrative,Destroy All Neighborscharms with inventive special effects and infectious cast chemistry.
Regressionboasts a pair of eminently likable leads -- neither of whom are able to dislodge the movie from the mire of psychological thriller mediocrity.
The cracks continue to show in Coppola's directorial style, butThe Outsidersremains a blustery, weird, and fun adaptation of the classic novel.

A domestic nightmare that draws its most profound scares from Sally Hawkins' deranged performance,Bring Her Backis an exemplary chiller that reaffirms directors Danny and Michael Philippou as modern masters of horror.
While its social commentary relies on basic ingredients,The Menuserves up black comedy with plenty of flavor.
Awkward tonal shifts and a sluggish pace plagueInnocent Blood-- a horror-comedy with anemic scares and laughs.
A visually tacky sequel that lumbers along with a plodding pulse and lacking the thematic resonance that distinguished its source material,Return to Silent Hillgets lost in the haze.
Reminiscent of Hitchcock and Chabrol,The Page Turneris elegant yet suspenseful, a revenge potboiler of a high degree.
Although it's often less than scary and parts of the story don't bear scrutiny,Knock at the Cabinis a thought-provoking chiller and upper-tier Shyamalan.
Neither awful enough to suck nor sharp enough to bite,Dracula Untoldmisses the point of its iconic character's deathless appeal.
A small film brimming with big ideas,Things Will Be Differenteffectively teases the brain while shredding the nerves.
Sergio Castellitto's latest film has a heavy dose of melodrama and is populated with unlikable characters.
Dark and bleak, the "kick-ass" performances, especially Nolte's "effective" portrayal of an abused soul, is the reason to see this film.
Beastplays like bleak poetry, unfurling its psychological thrills while guided by its captivating leads and mesmerizing, visceral visuals.
Campy comedy that squeaks by on its charms.
Tigers Are Not Afraiddraws on childhood trauma for a story that deftly blends magical fantasy and hard-hitting realism - and leaves a lingering impact.
In spite of its chortle-worthy premise and dated special effects,The Blobremains a prime example of how satisfying cheesy B-movie monster thrills can be.
We Summon the Darknessmakes the most of its rather pedestrian plot with palpable affection for genre formula, an appealing cast, and a sharp sense of humor.
Compelling performances from an outstanding cast helpDriventake audiences on an enjoyably stranger-than-fiction joyride.
Molly never really elevates above uninspired, cliche-ridden moments.
There's a new sheriff in town, and this one bites:Wolfcoppacks a silly punch with just enough fun to rip the laughter right out of you.
This satire on the art world is at times both clever and shallow, but its top-notch cast generates plenty of goodwill.
A Nightmare on Elm Street 3: Dream Warriorsoffers an imaginative and surprisingly satisfying rebound for a franchise already starting to succumb to sequelitis.
A welcome return for director Richard Stanley,Color Out of Spacemixes tart B-movie pulp with visually alluring Lovecraftian horror and a dash of gonzo Nicolas Cage.
Unapologetically silly and all the more entertaining for it,M3GANis the rare horror-comedy that delivers chuckles as effortlessly as chills.
Even with its disorienting leaps of logic and structure,Jacob's Ladderis an engrossing, nerve-shattering experience.
Night Shiftbristles with pitch-perfect laughs thanks to Ron Howard's snappy direction and a side-splitting turn from Michael Keaton.
Australia at its most sublime,The Turningblends top-tier talent with great views and winding narrative roads.
A dark teen comedy with an explosive twist,Spontaneousreaffirms Katherine Langford as a rising star -- and marks debuting director Brian Duffield as a filmmaker to watch.
Blackthorninvites comparisons to a classic Western -- and survives, thanks largely to a charismatic performance by a well-chosen Sam Shepard.
Halloween: The Curse of Michael Myerstrades the simple, brutal effectiveness of the original for convoluted mysticism, with disastrously dull results.
A message movie in a hard horror shell,Hatchingperches between beauty and revulsion -- and establishes director Hanna Bergholm as a bright new talent.
ABCs of Death 2delivers some inventively gory thrills, offering a surprising (albeit still somewhat uneven) upgrade over its predecessor.
Part documentary, part thriller,The Nightmareworks just well enough in both respects to deliver a uniquely disturbing viewing experience.
The Wailingdelivers an atmospheric, cleverly constructed mystery whose supernatural thrills more than justify its imposing length.
The Last Matineeis a blood-soaked homage to classic slasher cinema that delivers thrills, style, and spectacular gore.
Traumatikaoffers stylish, dread-soaked horror with harrowing visuals and sound, but its scattered plotting can make it feel like a collage of nightmares rather than a fully formed narrative.
Firestarter's concept hews too closely to other known Stephen King adaptations, though it's got nice special effects (including scenery-chewing George C. Scott).
Lacking the chilling suspense of the originalThe Strangersand proving to be just unpleasant, thisChapter 1closes the book on itself.
Campy and creepy,Suitable Fleshdelivers visceral, '80s-inspired horror topped off by game performances from Barbara Crampton and Heather Graham.
Its gruesome scares may nauseate more than thrill, butRabidbites into its story with distinct sophistication and thought-provoking themes.
Grindhousedelivers exhilarating exploitation fare with wit and panache, improving upon its source material with feral intelligence.

Catfishmay tread the line between real-life drama and crass exploitation a little too unsteadily for some viewers' tastes, but its timely premise and tightly wound mystery make for a gripping documentary.
Gracie Gillam's four-star performance helpsSuperhostroll out a blood red carpet for horror fans seeking delightfully grim accommodations.
Sinister 2has a few ingredients that will be familiar to fans of the original; unfortunately, in this slapdash second installment, none of them are scary anymore.
Stocked with talented stars but lacking any clear idea of what to do with them,No Such Thingis ultimately far too uneven to recommend.
Though it ultimately sacrifices some mystery in the name of gory thrills,Candymanis a nuanced, effectively chilling tale that benefits from an interesting premise and some fine performances.
Never aiming higher than threadbare jokes and offensive attempts at politically incorrect humor,Transylmaniais a vampire comedy that truly sucks.

A bona fide classic of midnight cinema, Tommy Wiseau's misguided masterpiece subverts the rules of filmmaking with a boundless enthusiasm that renders such mundanities as acting, screenwriting, and cinematography utterly irrelevant. You will never see a football the same way again.
Featuring hammy performances and bland characters,Solois an all too straightforward actioner that's both predictable and instantly forgettable.
Like Mestylishly explores some interesting ideas, even if they're too often consigned to the background in favor of more ordinary violence and gore.
Smartly constructed and powerfully acted,Hounds of Lovesatisfies as a psychological thriller with a few nasty surprises -- and marks writer-director Ben Young as a promising talent.
A werewolf revenge flick with bite,I Am Lisais a vibrant and intellectually nimble journey into female empowerment.
Audiences may attend to witness Peter O'Toole's Oscar-worthy performance, but they'll also be treated to a humane, tender exploration of maturing with both dignity and irreverence.
Night Shiftbristles with pitch-perfect laughs thanks to Ron Howard's snappy direction and a side-splitting turn from Michael Keaton.
Beautiful scenery and cinematography can't saveTuristasfrom its wooden acting and stale and predictable plot.
Home Alone's uneven but frequently funny premise, stretched unreasonably thin, is buoyed by Macaulay Culkin's cute performance and strong supporting stars.
A crowd-pleasing tribute to the magic of silent cinema,The Artistis a clever, joyous film with delightful performances and visual style to spare.
This HK actioner oozes with style.
Loud, incoherent, and dramatically listless,I, Frankensteinis a remarkably dull fantasy adventure that fails to generate much excitement or interest in its characters.
Southbounddoesn't entirely avoid the jarring shifts common to anthology films, but thanks to some thrilling twists and turns, this horror road movie is a surprisingly smooth ride.
A Dark Songoffers atmospheric, unsettling horror -- and marks writer-director Liam Gavin as one to watch.
With engaging human stories anchoring the action,Godzilla Minus Oneis one kaiju movie that remains truly compelling between the scenes of mass destruction.
Consistently unnerving as it cycles through a series of shorts that are more treat than trick,V/H/S Halloweenis one of the most purely enjoyable cassettes in the franchise yet.
Although director Zoe Lister-Jones has forged a new path for the weirdos of today,The Craft: Legacy's spells may only enchant fans of the original.
Waiting... is a gross-out comedy that is more gross than comic.
P2is full of gore, but low on suspense, featuring a cat-and-mouse plot has been done many times before.
A muddled brain-teaser, Stay has a solid cast and innovative visuals but little beneath the surface.
Director Gulager makes the most of what he's given; the resultingFeastoffers up some surprisingly tasty -- if far from nourishing -- morsels.
Waiting... is a gross-out comedy that is more gross than comic.
Wish Uponis neither scary nor original, but its fundamental flaws as a horror movie may make it destination viewing for after-midnight camp genre enthusiasts.
Starry Eyespokes Hollywood's seedy underbelly to produce a refreshingly original horror story led by a breakout performance from Alex Essoe.
Bamboozledis too heavy-handed in its satire and comes across as more messy and overwrought than biting.
Featuring groundbreaking animation, stunning visuals, and a talented cast,Beowulfhas in spades what more faithful book adaptations forget to bring: pure cinematic entertainment.
Playing against type withNightmare Alley, Tyrone Power and Edmund Goulding deliver some of their best work in a carnival-set noir unafraid to showcase true despair.
Cold Skinexerts a strong visual pull, even if all that icy atmosphere isn't a truly satisfying substitute for a story that dares to go somewhere different.
It's difficult to make a persuasive argument forThe Mummyas any kind of meaningful cinematic achievement, but it's undeniably fun to watch.
Love at First Bitecould use some more warm-blooded barbs to liven up its undead comedy, but George Hamilton's campy charisma gives the prince of darkness some welcome pizazz.
The opening's got a great fiery explosion and Stallone puts in another earnest, sympathetic performance, but all else inDaylightfeels designed to annoy the audience into submission.
Destroyer's grueling narrative is as uncompromising as Nicole Kidman's central performance, which adds extra layers to a challenging film that leaves a lingering impact.
A Nightmare on Elm Street 4: The Dream Mastermarks a relative high point in this franchise's bumpy creative journey, although the original remains far superior.
While bolstered by strong performances from Sam Rockwell and Anjelica Huston,Chokestruggles to capture the tone of Chuck Palahniuk's novel.
Relying on psychological tension rather than overt violence and gore,1408is a genuinely creepy thriller with a strong lead performance by John Cusack.
Never veering too far from the usual Hammer trappings,Dracula, Prince of Darknesscasts an effectively vicious vampire yarn with its chilling atmosphere and spirited cast of characters.
Though intentionally campy,Zombie Stripperssuffers from poor execution, and never rises above its silly premise.
Director John Irvin wastes the efforts of a tremendously talented cast, making this oneGhost Storythat deserves to be drowned out by boos.
The Intervention's familiar plot is enlivened by complex character dynamics and a likable cast.
A bone-chilling body horror,Hueseraoffers genre fans a twisted take on What to Expect When You're Expecting.
Jeruzalem's setting adds an interesting twist to this found-footage horror outing, even if the end result is still somewhat pedestrian.
A zany entry into the sentient murder toy genre,Benny Loves Youis an enjoyably macabre and clever horror-comedy that will have audiences rooting for its loveable villain.
A pleasant comedy with a great cast.
Alternately hilarious, gross, and simply diverting,Houseboundis the rare horror-comedy that delivers on both fronts.
Bereft of a single kernel of fear or excitement, thisChildren of the Cornsuggests the franchise is irrevocably lost in a maize of sub-mediocrity.
A convoluted and predictable plot overshadows the performances.
It's a Wonderful Knifetakes an enthusiastic stab at holiday-themed horror-comedy, even if it doesn't cut quite as deep as it should.
If its chilly sense of dread never quite becomes spine-tingling terror,Amuletremains smart, solidly disquieting fun for genre fans seeking slow-building horror.
Director John Cassavetes forgoes conventional style in this hard-bitten examination of a marriage, letting his actors tell the story through dialogue and theirFacesto refreshing effect.
Sharp, twisted, and darkly funny,12 Hour Shiftis an uncommonly clever heist caper with a refreshing female-focused twist.
Bride of Chuckyis devoid of any fright and the franchise has become tiresomely self-parodic, although horror fans may find some pleasure in this fourth entry's camp factor.
Reviewers say Turn It Up has a derivative feel, running through too many urban movie cliches.
A thoughtful look at the intricacies of girlhood in the modern age,Cutiesis a coming-of-age film that confronts its themes with poignancy and nuance.
Around the Bendsets a destination of earnest generational drama, but settles for the most derivative route to get there, veering off course into trite sentimentality.
Halloween Killsshould satisfy fans in search of brute slasher thrills, but in terms of advancing the franchise, it's a bit less than the sum of its bloody parts.
Its core idea is solid, butHeldsquanders most of its intriguingly nasty potential with lackluster overall execution.
Fun for fans even if it isn't as frightening as some of its predecessors,Annabelle Comes Homesuggests there's still some life left in theConjuringfranchise.
At nine films and counting, the line between Nicholas Sparks film fans and detractors is clear, andThe Best of Mewill change few minds on either side of the divide.
Sleightsubverts genre norms, delivering a smart and well-acted -- if ultimately somewhat frustrating -- magical mystery.
The laugh-free Dirty Love is a comedy dead zone -- it's aggressively crude and shoddily constructed.
The only thing this tired slasher flick may resurrect is nostalgia for when the genre was still fresh and scary.
A pointless shocker and societal allegory, a film whose imagery is so gruesome as to leave you scarred for life...or rolling your eyes for 100 minutes.
Buffy the Vampire Slayer's supernatural coming of age tale is let down by poor directing and even poorer plotting -- though Kristy Swanson and Paul Reubens' game performances still manage to slay.
Audiences may attend to witness Peter O'Toole's Oscar-worthy performance, but they'll also be treated to a humane, tender exploration of maturing with both dignity and irreverence.
Red State is an audacious and brash affair that ultimately fails to provide competent scares or thrills.
Illogical, tension-free, and filled with cut-rate special effects,Jaws: The Revengeis a sorry chapter in a once-proud franchise.
A derivative movie where the scares are few and things don't make much sense.
It's better than many of the other films M. Night Shyamalan has been associated with, butDevilnever gets more than a few low-budget thrills out of its fiendishly promising premise.
Some of its contents are more entertaining than others, but genre fans should still find thisScare Packagewell worth opening.
Beautiful to look at but a bit too respectfully crafted,On the Roaddoesn't capture the energy and inspiration of Jack Kerouac's novel.
Cujois artless work punctuated with moments of high canine gore and one wild Dee Wallace performance.
The Abandonedspends so much time setting up the creepy atmosphere, that it forgets to inhabit it with genuine spooks or scares.
Although many of the jokes have been done before, Anna Faris's bright performance and Gregg Araki's sharp direction makeSmiley Facemore than your average stoner comedy.
A handsomely told tale with an affecting performance from Lon Chaney, Jr.,The Wolf Manremains one of the classics of the Universal horror stable.
A mixture of gory slasher and sweet rom-com that ingeniously nails both formulas,Heart Eyesserves up a bloody valentine that'll make the heart skip a beat.
Charlize Theron gives a searing, deglamorized performance as real life serial killer Aileen Wuornos inMonster, an intense, disquieting portrait of a profoundly damaged soul.
There's some creepy imagery to be found, but not much in the way of logic or truly jarring scares.
Safe's eerie social satire and somewhat sterile stylization is balanced by comedic undertones and an impressive, understated performance from Julianne Moore.
An intriguing debut for writer-director Emmanuel Osei-Kuffour,Black Boxcompensates for a lack of surprises with strong performances and an emotionally rewarding story.
Cheap CG effects and laughable dialogue makeBlood and Chocolateworse than the usual werewolf flick.
Dark, violent, and drenched in dread,Goodnight Mommyis perfect for extreme horror enthusiasts -- or filmgoers who prefer to watch between splayed fingers.
The lurid display of camp soon turns tedious.
Killer Klowns from Outer Space's title promises darkly goofy fun -- and more often than not, the movie delivers.
The Exorcist: Believerearns points for trying to take the franchise back to its terrifying roots, but a lack of new ideas -- and scares -- make this an inauspicious start to a planned new trilogy.
As far as adaptations go,Nightbitchpacks more bark than bite as the stellar Amy Adams takes on motherhood and the neighborhood in a rather conventional romp.
Derivative and mostly uninspired,Devil's Dueadds little to either the found-footage or horror genres that it's content to mimic.
Restrained but disturbing,A Tale of Two Sistersis a creepily effective, if at times confusing, horror movie.
Director Christian Alvert has a certain stylish flair, but it's wasted onCase 39's frightless, unoriginal plot.
A suspenseful but perplexing thriller.
It may occasionally veer into discordant territory, butThe Auditionremains a darkly illuminating character study led by a forceful turn from Nina Hoss.
Exquisitely designed and fastidiously ornate, Masaki Kobayashi's ambitious anthology operates less as a frightening example of horror and more as a meditative tribute to Japanese folklore.
A faith-based film with a sweetly humanist grounding,Sightblinks at delivering much tonal nuance but delivers its true inspirational tale with moving conviction.
Filled with excellent performances, Ramin Bahrani's deft sophomore effort is a heartfelt, hopeful neorealist look at the people who live in the gritty underbelly of New York City.
Roland Emmerich's2012provides plenty of visual thrills, but lacks a strong enough script to support its massive scope and inflated length.
A reliance on cheap jump scares and well-worn tropes leaveHouse on Edena forgettable and creatively exhausted found footage experience.
BeyondThe Pale Doorlies an intriguing western/horror hybrid -- albeit one that struggles to fashion its halves into a compelling whole.
Getting the little details of true crime documentaries right before unleashing some truly unsettling twists,Strange Harvestreaps a bountiful crop of scares from its inventive structure.
Redlinehas plenty of bad acting, laughable dialogue, and luxury cars.
You just mightShutterin fear with this new-fangled Japanese horror film, but its approach is all too formulaic to leave a lasting impression.
Anchored by a wistful Robert Mitchum,Out of the Pastis an exemplary noir steeped in doom and sensuality.
Burialgets bogged down in its unnecessarily convoluted story, but it's also a B-movie with impressively weighty subtext.
Mr. SOUL!aims an overdue spotlight on a groundbreaking chapter in the history of American public television -- and the host who helped make it all happen.
It may not be the killer thrill ride you'd expect from an action movie with a cast of this caliber, butRedstill thoroughly outshines most of its big-budget counterparts with its wit and style.
The Invitationmakes brilliant use of its tension-rich premise to deliver a uniquely effective -- and surprisingly clever -- slow-building thriller.
Michael Shannon gives a powerhouse performance and the purposefully subtle filmmaking creates a perfect blend of drama, terror, and dread.
A muddled brain-teaser, Stay has a solid cast and innovative visuals but little beneath the surface.
Its classic horror aims exceed its blood-slicked grasp, butFalse Positiveworks its way sneakily under the skin.
M Night Shayamalan's The Sixth Sense is a twisty ghost story with all the style of a classical Hollywood picture, but all the chills of a modern horror flick.
Powerfully effective, this anti-fascist political thriller stands out as both high-conscience melodrama and high-tempo action movie.
A stylish and well-acted tonal blend,Medusaconfronts religious hypocrisy and sexism with invigorating anger.
A hard-hitting horror film whose surface-level frights are just as engaging as its thematic concerns,When Evil Lurksmarks a viscerally unsettling addition to the possession horror canon.
Reducing the once-terrifying Dream Reaper into a goofy caricature, this joyless climax will leave audiences hopingFreddystays dead.
A stark and emotionally resonant look at the life of a farm animal,Cowsimply yet insistently asks the viewer to reconsider their relationship with their food.
The Lords of Salemhas lots of atmospheric portent, but it's unfortunately short on scares.
The Transfigurationtells a quieter, more deliberately paced tale than genre fans might expect, but for those with the patience to let it sink in, it offers its own rewards.
With a plot as creaky as the boat, Ghost Ship fails to deliver the scares.
With little gore and a lot of creepy visuals,The Ringgets under your skin, thanks to director Gore Verbinski's haunting sense of atmosphere and an impassioned performance from Naomi Watts.
There's no quibbling with its ambitious aims, butUsersis more repetitive and less intelligent than it seems to think.
Da Sweet Blood of Jesushas no shortage of style, but it isn't enough to make this horror-tinged Spike Lee joint one of his best -- or worth recommending.
With effects work and solid direction from Stan Winston -- and Lance Henriksen adding welcome gravitas --Pumpkinheadis a creature feature that stands a cut above.
Badly written and directed, this poorly executed British horror suffers a glaring lack of scares and comprehensibility.
Body Snatchersmay not topple previous adaptions, but it boasts an effective sense of dread and strong characterizations.
Deft direction and strong performances from its all-female cast guideThe Descent, a riveting, claustrophobic horror film.
Skincould stand to go a bit deeper below its surface, but a worthy story and a committed performance from Jamie Bell make this timely drama well worth a watch.
The Skin I Live In lacks Almodovar's famously charged romance, replaced with a wonderfully bizarre and unpredictable detour into arthouse ick.
The Changeovermay not bite deep enough for more seasoned horror fans, but it's spooky enough for its younger target demographic.
Populated with characters as unpleasant as its sleazy storyline,The Loftis uninhabitable for all but the least demanding erotic thriller fans.
A Girl Walks Home Alone at Nightblends conventional elements into something brilliantly original -- and serves as a striking calling card for writer-director Ana Lily Amirpour.
Led by a powerful performance from Sônia Braga,Aquariususes a conflict between a tenant and developers to take an insightful look at the relationship between space and identity.

The music of the night has hit something of a sour note: Critics are calling the screen adaptation of Andrew Lloyd Webber's popular musical histrionic, boring, and lacking in both romance and danger. Still, some have praised the film for its sheer spectacle.
Fireworksseeks sparks in an ambitious blend of storytelling genres, but this misguided anime effort never truly takes flight.
Aside from its clichéd resolution,Disturbiais a tense, subtle thriller with a noteworthy performance from Shia LaBeouf.
The Exorcistrides its supernatural theme to magical effect, with remarkable special effects and an eerie atmosphere, resulting in one of the scariest films of all time.
There's Something in the Barnunwraps a wildly entertaining holiday rampage filled with witty jokes, creature feature charm, and eye-popping practical effects.
One of the weakest entries in the J-horror remake sweepstakes,One Missed Callis undone by bland performances and shopworn shocks.
Visually striking and aggressively confrontational,We are the Fleshmay prove as difficult to watch as it ultimately is to forget.
Loosely based on 1957's12 Angry Men, Nikita Mikhalkov's superbly acted12is clever and gripping like its predecessor, but with a distinctly Russian feel.
Luztakes a refreshingly unique approach to horror possession tropes, elevated by a chilly mood and minimalist scares.
A darkly amusing thriller that discomfits as it entertains,The Beta Testsatirizes Hollywood with savage flair.

With a deliciously wicked performance from Angelica Huston and imaginative puppetry by Jim Henson's creature shop, Nicolas Roeg's dark and witty movie captures the spirit of Roald Dahl's writing like few other adaptations.
Although its card is far from full,Bingo Helldelivers moderately effective socially conscious horror while suggesting greater things for director Gigi Saul Guerrero.
Using its low-budget effects and mockumentary method to great result,Paranormal Activityturns a simple haunted house story into 90 minutes of relentless suspense.
Marrowbone's effective setting and strong cast can't make up for thinly conceived characters and a story short on genuine scares.
Faithful to its literary source, this is imaginative, intelligent family entertainment.
Grim and gratifyingly ambitious,Where the Devil Roamsfinds the Adams Family tapping a Gothic vein filled with lingering horror.
Beautifully crafted with loosely drawn characters and a lilting, natural pace, Chris Smith'sThe Poolfeatures a universal message to which everyone can relate.
So scrappy that it feels as illicit as a book found in the woods,The Evil Deadis a stomach-churning achievement in bad taste that marks a startling debut for wunderkind Sam Raimi.
Synchronicsets off on an intriguingly idiosyncratic sci-fi journey that should satisfy fans of Aaron Moorhead and Justin Benson's earlier work.
Warm, funny, and populated by easily relatable characters,The Snapperis a compulsively watchable slice-of-life dramedy with much to recommend.
Rich atmosphere and a handful of genuinely disturbing moments are only sporadically enough to outweighThe Seeding's monotonous pace and lack of narrative depth.
Patrice Chéreau's exquisite rendering of Joseph Conrad'sThe Returnbrings underlying passions to surface in a long-suffering marriage.
TheV/H/Sseries shows no signs of the tape wearing out with this terrifying entry, boasting an assortment of shorts that'll sate the appetite of horror and sci-fi aficionados alike.
Led by an impressive Riley Keough performance,The Lodgeshould prove a suitably unsettling destination for fans of darkly atmospheric horror.
Matching up Bill Skarsgård and Anthony Hopkins in a battle of wits,Lockedis loaded with plenty of clever jolts even if it doesn't quite know where to park its story.
Down a Dark Hallis more stylish than scary, although its foreboding atmosphere may raise a few goosebumps among younger viewers.
Larry Drake's deranged performance as the titular doctor is just about all that distinguishesDr. Gigglesfrom its slasher brethren.
It's perhaps a bit tame and uninspiring, considering its subject matter, butPossessionmanages enough romance and period intrigue to satisfy most fans of its source material.
TheOperahouse location gives plenty to work with for director Dario Argento, who hits his decadently bloody high notes here.
Dreamscape mixes several genres -- horror, sci-fi, action -- and always maintains a sense of adventure and humor.
Creepylives up to its title with a suspenseful and thoroughly unsettling - not to mention well-acted - blend of crime procedural and domestic drama.
This psychological thriller's talented cast is undercut by leaden pacing and a problematic plot.
With an emphasis on dread over gore and an ending that leaves the door wide open for sequels,Oculuscould be just the first spine-tingling chapter in a new franchise for discerning horror fans.
Recapturing the magic of vintage adventure yarns,Bookwormis an imaginative gem with a wholesome heart.

While director Schrader's attempt at a literate, internal exposition on evil temptations and human sin is admirable, this prequel suffers from hit-and-miss psychological tension, poor visual effects, and weak writing -- an overambitious failure of a horror movie.
The Boogeymanmight fall short of its terrifying source material, but a spooky atmosphere and some solid performances help keep the chills coming.
Uzumakiuses its creepy, David Lynch-inspired atmospherics to effectively build a sense of dread, but ultimately fails to do anything with it.
Anthony Hopkins is as excellent as ever, but he's no match forThe Rite's dawdling pace and lack of chills -- or Colin O'Donoghue's tentative performance in the leading role.
John Waters' musical ode to the teen rebel genre is infectious and gleefully camp, providing star Johnny Depp with the perfect vehicle in which to lampoon his pin-up image.
A hollow creature feature that suffers from CGI overload.
ThoughJoshuais ultimately too formulaic, its intelligence and suspenseful buildup heighten the overall creep factor.
Ravenoustries bringing cannibal horror into an Old West setting, ending up with an uneven blend that will fail to satisfy most fans of either genre.
If you're in the mood for a fun enough genre film that kills its holiday theme, look no further than the surprisingly astuteA Christmas Horror Story.
ThoughBlade IIoffers more of what worked in the original, its plot and character development appear to have been left on the cutting room floor.
Led by Anna Diop's strong central performance, the smartly disquietingNannyis a promising debut for writer-director Nikyatu Jusu.
Conjuring fear with its sense of encroaching unease instead of cathartic jolts,In Flamesis an exemplary horror film with something profound to say about the oppression of women.

Packed with grim, relentless set pieces and gleefully excessive gore,Dollymay not reinvent the slasher wheel, but it spins it with such chaotic energy that it becomes an unapologetically brutal celebration of horror's most unhinged extremes.
Nina Forevertests the limits of the rom-com with a decidedly unorthodox triangle that's as diabolically original as it is daringly dark.
An unnecessary remake that's more gory and less scary than the original.
It's perhaps a bit tame and uninspiring, considering its subject matter, butPossessionmanages enough romance and period intrigue to satisfy most fans of its source material.
Pay the Ghosttakes a weak stab at supernatural horror, but ultimately, it's only viewers who will pay for watching this sloppily assembled picture.
Raising Caindoesn't rank with Brian De Palma's best work, but John Lithgow's spellbinding split-personality performance makes this thriller hard to dismiss.
The Exorcistrides its supernatural theme to magical effect, with remarkable special effects and an eerie atmosphere, resulting in one of the scariest films of all time.
Thrillingly provocative and original,Titanereaffirms writer-director Julia Ducournau's delightfully disturbing vision.
Boys from County Hellstands out as an uncommonly good time in the crowded vampire genre -- and proves the Irish countryside is a fine setting for slaying the undead.
Harmlessly hokey yet never much more than mediocre,Hocus Pocusis a muddled family-friendly effort that fails to live up to the talents of its impressive cast.
It isn't without its fun moments, butMeg 2: The Trenchsuffers from a disjointed story that drifts for too long before finally delivering a few campy thrills.
Though melodramatic at times, Together is heartwarming and well-acted.
The Watcher has Keanu Reeves cast against type, but the movie is short on thrills, suspense, and believability.
Slapface's uneven execution is offset by the thoughtful themes that fuel the monstrous developments in its character-driven horror story.
Visually and sonically distinctive,You'll Never Find Meheightens its spooky atmosphere and claustrophobic setting with a story that'll keep viewers guessing.
Smart and suspenseful,CAMis a techno-thriller that's far more than the sum of its salacious parts -- and an outstanding showcase for Madeline Brewer in the leading role.
The Girl with All the Giftsgrapples with thought-provoking questions without skimping on the scares -- and finds a few fresh wrinkles in the well-worn zombie horror genre along the way.
Brainy and bloody in equal measure,One Cut of the Deadreanimates the moribund zombie genre with a refreshing blend of formal daring and clever satire.
Ouija: Origin of Evilswerves its franchise's planchette unexpectedly to YES with a surprisingly scary and dramatically satisfying follow-up to its lackluster predecessor.

With a deliciously wicked performance from Angelica Huston and imaginative puppetry by Jim Henson's creature shop, Nicolas Roeg's dark and witty movie captures the spirit of Roald Dahl's writing like few other adaptations.
Stuffed to the gills with memorable shocks and a thrillingly unhinged heel turn by Jai Courtney,Dangerous Animalswill be irresistible chum for horror aficionados.
The messy Pumpkin wastes its premise by not making the satire sharp enough.
While it boasts an impressive cast, striking visuals, and an effective mood,Flatlinersnever quite jolts its story to life.
Bad Milo!sets some deliriously low expectations with its gross premise -- and then manages to match most of them in sick, entertaining style.
A brutal and effective British hoodie-horror that, despite the clichés, stays on the right side of scary.
Eerie and satirical,Motel Hellhas no vacancy when it comes to low-brow horror gags.
ThatThe Good Nightis the work of a first-time writer/director is apparent, as the story feels disjointed and never connects with the audience.
While providing several top-notch action scenes,The Kingdomultimately collapses under the weight of formula and muddled politics.
It isn't terribly deep, but it's witty and undeniably charming, and the cast is obviously having fun.
Neither scary nor creepy.
The Boycould have gone in any number of scary or interesting directions, but instead settles for usual jump scares scattered throughout a pedestrian plot.
Alien: Covenantdelivers another satisfying round of close-quarters deep-space terror, even if it doesn't take the saga in any new directions.
Capturing a media frenzy in shocking, eye-opening fashion, this documentary has its eyes on the truth and a commitment to counter the fearmongering spread by some who believeSatan Wants You.
Arguably Bergman's finest film,Personaexplores the human condition with intense curiosity, immense technical skill, and beguiling warmth.
It's all been done before, and done better.
Carnival of Soulsoffers delightfully chilling proof that when it comes to telling an effective horror story, less can often be much, much more.
Bewitched is haunted by scattered laughs and a lack of direction.
The premise grows too ridiculous to take seriously.
Like other video game adapations, Resident Evil is loud, violent, formulaic, and cheesy.
The Curse of Audrey Earnshawcompensates for a certain amount of frustrating narrative drift with solid performances and a strong command of mood.
Sanctumis beautifully photographed, and it makes better use of 3-D technology than most, but that doesn't make up for its ham-handed script and lifeless cast.
Despite a decent cast, subpar acting and a contrived plot disappointed reviewers.
Betty White's delightful supporting turn may be worth the price of admission alone, butLake Placidis swamped by a smarmy script and inability to deliver on the creature feature mayhem.
Expanding the franchise with some effective twists,Bird Box Barcelonalacks some of its predecessor's creepy tension but remains occasionally involving in its own right.
Jeepers Creepers 2 is competently made, but it doesn't have the scares of the original.
The Last Voyage of the Demeterfinds a fresh angle on Dracula's oft-told tale, although lackluster execution often undercuts the story's claustrophobic tension.
Despite occasional detours into surprisingly dark territory,Spreadoverall is an ineffectual celebration of vacuous Los Angeles high life rather than a deconstruction of it.
Roman Polanski's first English film follows a schizophrenic woman's descent into madness, and makes the audience feel as claustrophobic as the character.
Stylish and gripping,Detouroffers further proof that a patsy and a femme fatale often add up to a satisfying story.
The Invasionis slickly made, but it lacks psychological insight and thrills.
The best movie to star both the King and JFK.
Bringing Lulu Wilson back for another round of whooping on bad guys, thisWrathis oneBeckywith the good sequel.
Elevated by Mads Mikkelsen and Sophie Sloan's magnetic chemistry,Dust Bunnyis a dazzlingly imaginative and stylish feature debut from director Bryan Fuller.

This Must Be the Placemay feature too many wayward detours to satisfy some passengers, but Sean Penn's beguiling performance and Paolo Sorrentino's scenic visuals make this a road trip worth following.
Unsettling and compelling in equal measure,My Heart Can't Beat Unless You Tell It Tocasts a visually striking and thought-provoking spell.
A low budget thriller with some intense moments.
Eric Bana's performance as the charming but twisted Chopper is the highlight of this disturbing portrait about Australia's notorious author/criminal.
Drenched in dark atmosphere and bolstered with some real emotion underneath all the chills,The Hallowsuggests a wonderfully horrifying future for director Corin Hardy.
Trilogy of Terrordelivers a twisty anthology of macabre stories driven by Karen Black's captivating multi-character performance and whirlwind visuals.
Despite a talented cast and some reliably pleasant interplay between Jon Favreau and Vince Vaughn,Couples Retreatleaves viewers stranded in an arid, mirthless comedy.
Devoid of believable characters or convincing visual effects, this may beThe Darkest Hourfor the careers of all involved.
A reverent adaptation of Thomas Hardy's novel,Tessmarries painterly cinematography and unhurried pacing to create an epic ode to perseverance.
The Red Ballooninvests the simplest of narratives with spectacular visual inventiveness, making for a singularly wondrous portrait of innocence.
An urgent warning against nuclear conflict,Threadsis a chilling hypothetical that achieves visceral horror with its matter-of-fact presentation of an apocalypse.
Despite solid production values and an intriguing premise,Constantinelacks the focus of another spiritual shoot-em-up:The Matrix.
ThoughFracture's plot is somewhat implausible, the onscreen face-off between Gosling and Hopkins overshadows any faults.
Janehonors its subject's legacy with an absorbing, beautifully filmed, and overall enlightening look at her decades of invaluable work.
The Cabin in the Woodsis an astonishing meta-feat, capable of being funny, strange, and scary -- frequently all at the same time.
Halloween 4: The Return Of Michaels Myersmay bring the series' masked killer back into the fold, but fails to offer the visceral scares and inventiveness of the original.
Too Latepays homage to its genre inspirations affectionately enough to beg forgiveness for relying on them so heavily.
An implausible, overheated potboiler that squanders a stellar cast, Twisted is a clichéd, risible whodunit.
It's perhaps a bit tame and uninspiring, considering its subject matter, butPossessionmanages enough romance and period intrigue to satisfy most fans of its source material.
Another stale American remake of a successful Japanese horror film,Pulsebypasses the emotional substance of the original and overcompensates with pumped-up visuals and every known horror cliche.
Chain Reactionsrewards horror fans and cinephiles alike with a thoughtful, unpretentious meditation on the cultural and artistic impact of one of cinema's most influential works.
Neither scary nor funny, The Haunted Mansion is as lifeless as the ghosts in the movie.
The Heatis predictable, but Melissa McCarthy is reliably funny and Sandra Bullock proves a capable foil.
Witty and restrained but still taut and funny, thisPontypoolis a different breed of low-budget zombie film.
Stylish with a sadistic streak,Piercingpairs gripping lead performances with a smartly macabre story that delivers unpredictable thrills.
It's admittedly uneven, butWho Invited Thembenefits from writer-director Duncan Birmingham's playful approach to genre tropes.
Jakob's Wifegives genre legend Barbara Crampton an opportunity to carry an old-school horror story -- and she bloody well delivers.
The Long Walkmay require patience from some viewers, but they'll be rewarded with a thoughtful and alluringly atmospheric ghost story.
The over-the-top gore, campy acting, and dim cinematography may be part ofHatchet's self-described old-school ethos, but irony alone can't sustain a horror film.
Halloween Ends-- for now, anyway -- with a frequently befuddling installment that's stabbed, slashed, and beaten by a series of frustrating missed opportunities.
The Belko Experimentoffers a few moments of lurid fun for genre enthusiasts, but lacks enough subversive smarts to consistently engage once the carnage kicks in.
Jason goes to the future, but the story is still stuck in the past.
A high-concept movie that plays out like a mediocre TV sitcom.
Identity is a film that will divide audiences -- the twists of its plot will either impress or exasperate you.
Ichi The Killer is a thoroughly shocking gorefest that will surely entertain those with strong stomachs and a penchant for brutal violence.
Nocebostruggles to consistently engage, but unsettling atmosphere and interesting ideas make this thriller difficult to dismiss outright.
The Conjuring 2can't help but lose a bit of its predecessor's chilly sting through familiarity, but what remains is still a superior ghost story told with spine-tingling skill.
Continuing the trend of schlocky puerile remakes,Peter Pan's Neverland Nightmareguts a beloved character for low-brow horror that never manages to take flight.
The Searchersis an epic John Wayne Western that introduces dark ambivalence to the genre that remains fashionable today.
Although it adds little to the long tradition of Dickens adaptations,Spiritedis so genially overstuffed that it's easy to at least intermittently enjoy.
An incoherent and overly long creature feature.
Cruelly clever with some unforgettably gory set pieces,The Monkeyreaffirms director Osgood Perkins' horror bona fides while revealing he also has a surprising -- albeit sick -- sense of humor.
If its story's mysteries are ultimately less compelling than they might seem,Enys Men's retro aesthetic and intriguingly abstract visuals make this a chilly treat for horror fans.
Filled with style and dark, lurid energy,The Crowis an action-packed visual feast that also has a soul in the performance of the late Brandon Lee.
Sarah Paulson is as arresting as ever, but a dearth of incident and scares ultimately robsHold Your Breathof long-awaited release.
It isn't particularly subtle, butThe Purge: Election Year's blend of potent jolts and timely themes still add up to a nastily effective diversion.
Supernatural horror of a refreshingly subtle vintage,Satoruses one family's fight against demonic possession to cast its own dark, stubborn spell.
Saw II is likely to please the gore-happy fans of the original, though it may be too gruesome for those not familiar with first film's premise.
Takenis undeniably fun with slick action, but is largely a brainless exercise.
Impeccably cast and smartly written,Bodies Bodies Bodiesis an uncommonly well-done whodunit.
The Watcher has Keanu Reeves cast against type, but the movie is short on thrills, suspense, and believability.
Despite being blessed with Jena Malone in the lead,Consecrationserves up Catholic-themed horror that's far more hoary than hallowed.
Alice, Sweet Aliceoffsets some of its more ludicrous elements with campy slasher thrills and a dazzlingly macabre atmosphere.
Much darker and more sensitive than the sequels it spawned,First Bloodis a thrilling survival adventure that takes full advantage of Sylvester Stallone's acting skills.
Although its subject matter may be hard to stomach,Bones and Allproves a deeply romantic and thought-provoking treat.
While there are some built-in scares, the movie is muddled and unsatisfying.
The Front Roomgets some mileage out of Kathryn Hunter's memorably grotesque turn as an in-law from hell, but the scatological scares in this family squabble are more off-putting than frightening.
Hugois an extravagant, elegant fantasy with an innocence lacking in many modern kids' movies, and one that emanates an unabashed love for the magic of cinema.
Although its story can feel circuitous and a bit baggy,Brooklyn 45's character-driven period horror is elevated by some excellent performances.
The Messengersis an atmospheric but derivative rip-off of countless other horror movies.
Anchored by Yllka Gashi's outstanding performance,Hiveleads viewers on one woman's fact-based quest for self-determination in a patriarchal society.
Sarah Paulson is as arresting as ever, but a dearth of incident and scares ultimately robsHold Your Breathof long-awaited release.
Shaped by Todd Haynes' deft direction and powered by a strong cast led by Cate Blanchett and Rooney Mara,Carollives up to its groundbreaking source material.
Leave No Tracetakes an effectively low-key approach to a potentially sensationalistic story -- and further benefits from brilliant work by Ben Foster and Thomasin McKenzie.
Snatchers' practical effects and strong writing come together to create a sci-fi comedy that's fun, frenetic, and at times, freakin' crazy (in the best way).
The consistency isn't always smooth, butSound of Violence's blend of character study and horror marks writer-director Alex Noyer as an ambitious, visually distinctive filmmaker.
One of legendary director Akira Kurosawa's most acclaimed films, Rashomon features an innovative narrative structure, brilliant acting, and a thoughtful exploration of reality versus perception.
While it may lack the twisted pizzazz of the franchise it chronicles,Living with Chuckyis a mostly engaging primer on everyone's favorite homicidal plaything.
Despite Hitch's predictability, Will Smith and Kevin James win praise for their solid, warmhearted performances.
A messy horror-comedy hybrid that makes poor use of a talented cast,Tone-Deafaims for social commentary, but tumbles down the generation gap.
House Partyis a light, entertaining teen comedy with an infectious energy.
Stars Heath Ledger and Abbie Cornish look better than they should as heroin addicts, and their characters are too absorbed and self-pitying to be totally compelling.
Carried by Kiersey Clemons' performance,Sweetheartbalances smart subtext and social commentary against effective genre thrills.
AlthoughBrightburndoesn't fully deliver on the pitch-black promise of its setup, it's still enough to offer a diverting subversion of the superhero genre.
Calvaire (The Ordeal)has a certain amount of grim potential, but loses effectiveness by too often mistaking disturbing gore for genuine horror.
Just becauseFameis a well-acted musical doesn't mean it flinches against its surprisingly heavy topics.
Smart, well-acted, and above all scary,Coming Home in the Darkfinds first-time director James Ashcroft making his mark with a white-knuckle ride for horror fans.
The over-the-top gore, campy acting, and dim cinematography may be part ofHatchet's self-described old-school ethos, but irony alone can't sustain a horror film.
Offering just about everything longtime fans could hope for while still managing to carry the franchise forward,Evil Dead Riseis all kinds of groovy.
An artsy horror flick, Wendigo effectively creates an eerie atmosphere.
Without compelling characters or heart,Godzillastomps on everything that made the original (or any monster movie worth its salt) a classic.
Unsettling and absorbing in equal measure,Possumpresents a dark character study rich with rewards for fans of chilling genre fare.
Led by Anna Diop's strong central performance, the smartly disquietingNannyis a promising debut for writer-director Nikyatu Jusu.
Proof that there's still life in the found-footage gimmick,Deadstreamis a scarily good bit of B-movie fun.
Old Dolls can learn new tricks: This little murderer with a facelift is sillier and better than ever thanks to Don Mancini'sCult of Chucky.
With a hard fantasy story that stands in service of its eye-catching animation,The Spine of Nightis a distinctive treat for genre enthusiasts.
Dead Snow 2: Red vs. Deadexpands the original's canvas without sacrificing any of its bloody fun, adding up to a sequel that fans of the first are bound to enjoy.
Disney's take on the Victor Hugo classic is dramatically uneven, but its strong visuals, dark themes, and message of tolerance make for a more-sophisticated-than-average children's film.
Vivariummay confound almost as often as it intrigues, but this well-acted sci-fi/horror hybrid has interesting ideas -- and explores them with style.
Mary Shelley's Frankensteinis ambitious and visually striking, but the overwrought tone and lack of scares make for a tonally inconsistent experience.
A hollow creature feature that suffers from CGI overload.
Flashes of suspense hint at the horror movie it might have been, butNight's Endis ultimately too dull and repetitious to recommend.
Lon Chaney's typically strong performance -- and terrifying makeup -- casts an eerie spell in this solid detective thriller.
A funnier follow-up with a sci-fi bent,Happy Death Day 2Uisn't as fiendishly fresh as its predecessor, but fans of the original may still find this a sequel worth celebrating.
This Danish feast may leave you wanting more, butWhen Animals Dreamremains a solid attempt to redefine female adolescence through a lycanthropic lens.
Anna and the Apocalypsefinds fresh brains and a lot of heart in the crowded zombie genre - not to mention a fun genre mashup populated by rootable characters.
Balancing heart and humor against outstanding work from a talented cast,C.R.A.Z.Y.proves the coming-of-age formula can still produce powerful results.
A bonkers riff on the lengths people will go to for connection,Dead Lover's singular sensibility can be an acquired taste but writer-director Grace Glowicki's vision is admirably one of a kind.
Viewers immune to its star's charms won't find it much of a treat, butHubie Halloweenis sweet enough to satisfy fans of Adam Sandler's antics.
Sporadically suspenseful,Barbariansis too tonally indecisive to consistently make its most important points.
WhileWe Need to Do Somethingcan feel as unfocused as its title, it offers eerily timely genre thrills, soaked in claustrophobic dread.
Blood Work is a routine, but competently made thriller marred by lethargic pacing.
Less a return toScream's roots than a disappointing creative regression, this seventh entry draws little blood with its dull knife of a script.
Lacking scares or psychological insight,Captivityis a distasteful entry in the 'torture porn' subgenre.
At the Devil's Doorhas no shortage of creepy style -- unfortunately, that isn't enough to distract from an uninspired story that never capitalizes on its potential.
Below is a creepy, claustrophobic exercise in style.
The Wolf of Snow Hollowtreads somewhat unsteadily between horror and comedy, but writer-director-star Jim Cummings' unique sensibilities make for an oddly haunting hybrid.
Arachnophobia may not deliver genuine chills, but it's an affectionate, solidly built tribute to Hollywood's classic creature features.
The Secrets We Keephandles its more serious themes indelicately, but manages to offer some entertaining -- albeit implausible -- B-movie thrills.
Unsettling and absorbing in equal measure,Possumpresents a dark character study rich with rewards for fans of chilling genre fare.
An implausible, overheated potboiler that squanders a stellar cast, Twisted is a clichéd, risible whodunit.
David Lynch's surrealEraserheaduses detailed visuals and a creepy score to create a bizarre and disturbing look into a man's fear of parenthood.
Paranormal Activity: Next of Kingives the long-running found-footage franchise a new level of visual polish; unfortunately, effective scares are few and far between.
The Autopsy of Jane Doesubverts the gruesome expectations triggered by its title to deliver a smart, suggestively creepy thriller that bolsters director André Ovredal's growing reputation.
There's more vapid action and less story in Underworld Awakening than previous installments, making the whole affair feel inconsequential.
Night of the Zoopocalypsedelightfully threads the needle as a child-friendly introduction to horror, delivering a gentle amount of good scares while dazzling the eye with its neon hues.
Led by an arresting Sam Worthington performance,Fracturedis a reasonably diverting mystery with enough thrills to make up for a familiar story.
Meatballsis a summer camp comedy with few surprises, but Bill Murray's riffing adds a spark that sets it apart from numerous subpar entries in a frequently uninspired genre.
For the Sake of Viciousdelivers on the gory promise of its title with a no-holds-barred thriller that should delight fans of pulpy B-movie splatter.
