Paranormal Activity 2doesn't cover any new ground, but its premise is still scary -- and in some respects, it's a better film than the original.
A film as beautiful to contemplate as it is to behold,Soulproves Pixar's power to deliver outstanding all-ages entertainment remains undimmed.
Though it sacrifices some depth in its characterizations,From Beyondstands as a stunningly grotesque Lovecraft adaptation with a dazzling blend of chilling effects and brainy, cosmic horror.
The Dark and the Wickeddelivers on its title with an unsettling horror story whose deep dread and bleak outlook further compound its effective jolts.
Zombieland: Double Tapmakes up for a lack of fresh brains with an enjoyable reunion that recaptures the spirit of the original and adds a few fun twists.
Daniel Isn't Real, but the smart, stylish fun waiting for genre lovers in this well-acted suspense thriller is completely genuine.
Stylish yet hollow,The Hungeris a well-cast vampire thriller that mistakes erotic moments for a satisfying story.
Well-preformed, but it's nothing we haven't seen before.
Passion Playhas a terrific cast, but don't be fooled - the only real question at the heart of this misbegotten mystery is what its stars were thinking.
Child's Playoccasionally stumbles across its tonal tightrope of comedy and horror, but its genuinely creepy monster and some deft direction by Tom Holland makes this chiller stand out on the shelf.
The Condemnedis a morally ambiguous, exceedingly violent and mostly forgettable action film.
Grim and macabre,In My Mother's Skinis a bleakly effective blend of historical and supernatural horror.
A dim and predictable remake of an already dull slasher film, thisProm Nightfails to be memorable.
Ivan Reitman's refreshingly earnest political comedy benefits from an understated, charming script and a breezy performance by Kevin Kline.
Gwen's relentlessly grim outlook may wear on some viewers, but it remains a reasonably gripping, solidly assembled descent into atmospheric period horror.
Dare's sharp writing and appealing young cast are often enough to outweigh its jarringly jumbled narrative.
Arguably the first true horror film,The Cabinet of Dr. Caligariset a brilliantly high bar for the genre -- and remains terrifying nearly a century after it first stalked the screen.
Oh my god.
With death-defying action sequences and epic historic sweep,Herooffers everything a martial arts fan could ask for.
A yuletide nightmare full of familial angst and slithering scares,Await Further Instructionsis a genre treat that pretty much any horror fan will want in their stocking.
An entertaining PG detour for gore maestro Eli Roth,The House with a Clock in Its Wallsis a family-friendly blend of humor and horror with an infectious sense of fun.
George A. Romero's debut set the template for the zombie film, and features tight editing, realistic gore, and a sly political undercurrent.

Amusing as a satire of Americans abroad but less convincing as a horror hybrid,I Don't Understand Yougets some mileage from Andrew Rannells and Nick Kroll's chemistry but can't quite overcome its tonal language barrier.
Blood Quantumblends bloody horror with sociopolitical subtext, taking a fresh bite out of the crowded zombie genre in the bargain.
Making the most of its thin premise,Fidois an occasionally touching satire that provides big laughs and enough blood and guts to please gorehounds.
This Adam Sandler vehicle borrows shamelessly fromIt's A Wonderful LifeandBack To The Future, and fails to produce the necessary laughs that would forgive such imitation.
Heavy on mood and light on answers,Went Up the Hillis a heady ghost story that weaves an intoxicating spell for those patient enough to surrender to its languid rhythms.
American Carnageis an unsubtle but intermittently sharp horror-comedy whose macabre, politically charged satire is promising yet too thinly spread to fully cohere.

Evocative direction by Jacques Tourneur collides with the low-rent production values of exploitateer Val Lewton inI Walked with a Zombie, a sultry sleeper that's simultaneously smarmy, eloquent and fascinating.
The Strangershas a handful of genuinely scary moments, but they're not enough to elevate the end results above standard slasher fare.
A fiendishly goofy premise, strong performances, and a strong dose of winkingly meta humor helpVicious Fundeliver on its title.
Suspended Animationoffers some gory good fun for genre fans, even if the end results don't quite transcend their cheerfully shallow roots.
Paul Newman is at the peak of his powers as an attorney who never lived up to his potential inThe Verdict, supported by David Mamet's crackling script and Sidney Lumet's confident direction.
Despite the chemistry of its stars, Guess Who, a loose remake of Guess Who's Coming to Dinner, lacks the political relevance of the original.
The Babadookrelies on real horror rather than cheap jump scares -- and boasts a heartfelt, genuinely moving story to boot.
Narratively contrived and visually a mess,The Gallowssends viewers on a shaky tumble to the bottom of the found-footage horror barrel.
I'll See You in My Dreamswould be worth watching even if Blythe Danner's central performance was all it had going for it, but this thoughtful drama satisfies on multiple levels.
A creative and energetic adaptation of a Clive Barker short story, with enough scares and thrills to be a potential cult classic.
Packing a lot into its brief runtime,Jethicaanchors its supernatural thrills with the serious story of a truly terrifying stalker.
Influencersis a sharper, darker, and gleefully vicious sequel that skewers hollow influencer culture with stylish carnage and delicious black humor.
An uneasy blend of action and comedy,Date Nightdoesn't quite live up to the talents of its two leads, but Steve Carell and Tina Fey still manage to shine through most of the movie's flaws.
With an intriguing enough premise and horror legend Wes Craven on writer-director duty, the realShockerhere is just how lame the end results turned out to be.
Slightly stuck in genre convention, but mostly anIck-fest of horrific fun that oozes solid B-film trappings.
Its contents may be a bit busy and overly familiar, butNightbooksoffers a fun -- and actually fairly scary -- gateway to horror for younger viewers.
Deeply unnerving and surprisingly poignant,The Orphanageis an atmospheric, beautifully crafted haunted house horror film that earns scares with a minimum of blood.
Fans of the two horror franchises will enjoy this showdown. But for everyone else, it's the same old slice and dice.
It's often painful to watch, butRabbit Hole's finely written script and convincing performances make it worth the effort.
The Pestaims for zany comedy with social commentary, misses, and lands squarely at offensive misfire thanks to John Leguizamo's over-caffeinated performance and a script laden with stereotypes.
Knife + Heartwrings giallo-inspired thrills out of a boldly challenging story that defiantly succeeds on its own stylish merits.
Flux Gourmetisn't for all tastes, but viewers attuned to writer-director Peter Strickland's unique wavelength will find this horror-laced satire a savory delight.
While28 Weeks Laterlacks the humanism that made28 Days Latera classic, it's made up with fantastic atmosphere and punchy direction.
This dark psycho-thriller from Jennifer Lynch, is violent, sharp and baffling, but not to everyone's taste.
Despite Sandra Oh's typically strong performance and glimmers of potential from writer-director Iris K. Shim,Ummais a cliché-ridden missed opportunity.
Sloppy, vulgar, and manic,A Haunted House 2might be worth a chuckle or two, but mostly it's a string of pop culture references and crude gags that fail to hit their intended targets.
An eccentric, campy, technically impressive, and frightening picture, James Whale'sBride of Frankensteinhas aged remarkably well.
A slasher with flair and crafty patience for the kill,The Stylistmarks writer-director Jill Gevargizian as an uncommonly sharp genre filmmaker.
Zombeaversobviously isn't high art, but it delivers just enough of the gory insanity promised by the title to earn a recommendation for genre fans.
Relicratchets up its slowly building tension in an expertly crafted atmosphere of dread, adding up to an outstanding feature debut for director/co-writer Natalie Erika James.
A potent metaphor for toxic relationships,The Dead Thingunfolds slowly but proves haunting thanks to its eerie mood and Blu Hunt's committed performance.
Project Wolf Hunting's slight story takes a back seat to all the action -- and for fans of all-out cinematic mayhem, that's exactly as it should be.
Thanks to brilliant performances from Debra Winger and especially Anthony Hopkins,Shadowlandsis a deeply moving portrait of British scholar C.S. Lewis's romance with American poet Joy Gresham.
Monster Summeroffers some decent PG scares and an appealing cast, yet ultimately feels like a diluted, slightly clunky imitation of its genre influences.
Visually impressive, but this latest Ripper tale is dull and far from scary.
Led by a trio of strong performances,Arcadianblends family drama and post-apocalyptic horror to visceral, emotionally stirring effect.
Visually splendid and narratively satisfying,Tale of Talespacks an off-kilter wallop for mature viewers in search of something different.
Critics labeledBellaas a simplistic and mostly pedestrian, but positive word of mouth gave this tiny indie surprising theatrical legs.
Phantasm: Remasteredadds visual clarity to the first installment in one of horror's most enduring -- and endearingly idiosyncratic -- franchises.
Its restrained approach may frustrate horror fans seeking more visceral thrills, butThe Last Thing Mary Sawtells a haunting period tale.
Jacqueline Castel's feature debut is an arresting queer and lycanthropic coming of age story that longs for more, but delivers just enough panache to satisfy.
Boasting masterful cinematography to match its well-acted, wonderfully romantic storyline,Sunriseis perhaps the final -- and arguably definitive -- statement of the silent era.
Body at Brighton Rockleaves some of its thrilling potential untapped, but the end result remains a solidly crafted outing that should entertain horror fans.
Blessed with a nerve-jangling star turn by Naomi Scott, writer-director Parker Finn broadensSmile's conceit into a pop stardom nightmare that'll leave a rictus grin on horror fans' faces.
Complex, human characters and on-location shooting giveBeyond the Gatespalpable tension and urgency.
Whether it's being presented asD-ToxorEye See You, this Stallone starring vehicle is a slapdash thriller to actively avoid.
The Covenantplays out like a teen soap opera, full of pretty faces, wooden acting, laughable dialogue, and little suspense.
With Katharine Hepburn and the city of Venice glowing under David Lean's direction,Summertimeis a swooning romance for the ages.
Miscast and dramatically inert,Miralis a thin chronicle of geopolitical history that offers only shallow insight into the Israeli-Palestinian conflict and characters with even less depth.
A subtle, psychologically driven found footage,What Happened to Dorothy Bell?rises above genre conventions with its haunting exploration of inherited trauma and hidden family pain.
Like the best horror/comedies,Tucker & Dale vs. Evilmines its central crazy joke for some incredible scares, laughs, and -- believe it or not -- heart.
The First Wavepresents a starkly gripping snapshot of a crisis unfolding -- and its grim toll on those who put their lives on the line to fight it.
The Oneplays more like a video game than a movie and borrows freely from other, better sci-fi actioners, burying Jet Li's spectacular talents under heaps of editing and special effects.
This Is GWARis an entertaining and oddly inspiring documentary that vividly captures the band's grotesque artistry, wild performances, and candid personal stories.
28 Years Latertaps into contemporary anxieties with the ferocious urgency of someone infected with Rage Virus, delivering a haunting and visceral thrill ride that defies expectations.
A cool and hip grindhouse throwback,Planet Terroris an unpredictable zombie thrillride.
Dare's sharp writing and appealing young cast are often enough to outweigh its jarringly jumbled narrative.
It isn't Tim Burton's best work, butSleepy Hollowentertains with its stunning visuals and creepy atmosphere.
Vibrantly filmed and finely detailed,Memory Boxis an engaging depiction of one woman's reckoning with her past.
Clown in a Cornfielddoesn't reinvent the corn-maze, but its clever insights, subversion of expectations, and solid foundation help slash its way to the top.
The epitome of so-bad-it's-good cinema,Plan 9 From Outer Spaceis an unintentionally hilarious sci-fi "thriller" from anti-genius Ed Wood that is justly celebrated for its staggering ineptitude.
Elevated by writer-director Clive Barker's fiendishly unique vision,Hellraiseroffers a disquieting - and sadistically smart - alternative to mindless gore.
Hellbenderstirs a boiling cauldron of adolescent angst, serving up a coming-of-age story with dark horror overtones.
Three auteurs descend on the works of Poe, each putting on a ghoulish show -- adapting The Tomahawk Man's tales of dreams and fright, with Fellini's segment particularly out of sight.
Phantasm: Remasteredadds visual clarity to the first installment in one of horror's most enduring -- and endearingly idiosyncratic -- franchises.
Neither scary nor very funny, this misguided effort never lives up to its premise.
It boasts a talented cast, but Kimberly Peirce's "reimagining" of Brian De Palma's horror classic finds little new in the Stephen King novel -- and feels woefully unnecessary.
Frequently frightening even as it plays within the confines of a nearly 50-year-old film series, this prequel isThe First Omenof a bright future for the franchise in quite some time.
Arguably the first true horror film,The Cabinet of Dr. Caligariset a brilliantly high bar for the genre -- and remains terrifying nearly a century after it first stalked the screen.
Girluses one aspiring dancer's story as the framework for a poignant drama that approaches its difficult themes with fittingly alluring grace.
Wildling's feminist themes - and the ferocious Bel Powley performance that brings them to life - are often enough to make up for its narrative deficiencies.
Hellboy:The Crooked Manbuckles under its constraints resulting in an uneven, third-rate bag of all tricks and no treats.
Injecting a classic story with fresh innovation and social relevance,The Angry Black Girl and Her Monsteris a thrillingly assured feature debut for writer-director Bomani J. Story.
The Disappointments Roomlives down to its title with a thrill-free thriller that presumably left its stars filled with regret - and threatens to do the same for audiences.
This low budget British psycho-thriller packs plenty of atmospheric tension and suspense into it's slender running time.
Hannibal Risingreduces the horror icon to a collection of dime-store psychological traits.
Antebellumfails to connect its images with any meaning, making for a largely unpleasant experience lacking any substantial scares.
A Ghost Storydeftly manages its ambitious themes through an inventive, artful, and ultimately poignant exploration of love and loss.
A gleefully silly, puppet-filled homage to '80s creature features,Frankie Freakofully embraces its own absurdity to supply a brisk and irreverent dose of horror-comedy fun.
This dark psycho-thriller from Jennifer Lynch, is violent, sharp and baffling, but not to everyone's taste.
Bursting with visual style but suffering from a dearth of discernible narrative,The Strange Color of Your Body's Tearsis recommended only for giallo enthusiasts.
A Haunted Houseseeks to offend every sensibility, but it's greatest sin: not being funny!
There's an abundance of gore in this derivative horror movie, but little sense or wit.
John Lithgow's frighteningly unhinged performance reigns overThe Rule of Jenny Pen, a nasty chiller that's by turns monotonous and haunting.

Those unfamiliar with Alejandro Jodorowsky's style may find it overwhelming, butSanta Sangreis a provocative psychedelic journey featuring the director's signature touches of violence, vulgarity, and an oddly personal moral center.
Smart, funny, and disarmingly sweet,Matineeis a film that film buffs will love -- and might even convert some non-believers.
A Haunted Houseseeks to offend every sensibility, but it's greatest sin: not being funny!
In Sleeper, Woody Allen's madcap futurist comedy, practically each joke and one-liner hits it target.
As is often the case with anthology films,The ABCs of Deathis wildly uneven, with several legitimately scary entries and a bunch more that miss the mark.
Schlocky and gross but far from bad enough to be good,Argento's Dracula 3Dbites and sucks in all the wrong ways.
It boasts a talented cast, but Kimberly Peirce's "reimagining" of Brian De Palma's horror classic finds little new in the Stephen King novel -- and feels woefully unnecessary.
Horror Noire: A History of Black Horrormore than lives up to its title, offering a smart and entertaining overview of American film history through an overlooked lens.
Three Kings successfully blends elements of action, drama, and comedy into a thoughtful, exciting movie on the Gulf War.
A likable cast and strong set pieces giveThere's Someone Inside Your Housea lift, but they're outweighed by its messily misguided story.
The romantic leads are too obnoxious and self-centered to generate interest or sympathy.
A fun and stylish thriller if you can get past the contrivances.
A clever premise gets plenty of comic blood and violence, but its hampered by some questionable storytelling techniques from director Quentin Dupieux.
A clinical, maddening descent into the mind of a serial killer and a slowly unraveling hero, culminating with one of the scariest endings of all time.
The Darknessclumsily relies on an assortment of genre tropes, leaving only the decidedly non-frightening ghost of superior horror films in its wake.
It boasts a talented cast, but Kimberly Peirce's "reimagining" of Brian De Palma's horror classic finds little new in the Stephen King novel -- and feels woefully unnecessary.
It's far from the most toothless shark thriller, butGreat Whitelacks the killer storytelling instinct necessary to stay afloat.
Hot Spring Shark Attacktakes a bite out of its audience with an astute wack-fest that manages to float above its shortcomings.
George A. Romero's contribution to vampire lore contains the expected gore and social satire -- but it's also surprisingly thoughtful, and boasts a whopper of a final act.
A nerve-wracking continuation of its predecessor,A Quiet Place Part IIexpands the terrifying world of the franchise without losing track of its heart.
The Saint is watchable thanks to Kilmer and Shue, but the muddled screenplay stretches credulity.
Lesbian Vampire Killersstakes a claim to niche British humor, but ultimately succumbs to dreary twaddle.

Laverne Cox's fabulous portrayal of Frank N. Furter leads a strong ensemble effort, but the stars can't infuse this reimagining with enough energy, creativity, and quirk to makeTRHPS: Let's Do the Time Warp Againa worthwhile endeavor.
Times aren't desperate enough for this lame thriller to be called for.
Using an effectively creepy setting and a beguilingly evasive approach,Koko-Di Koko-Datakes a chilling look at the long tail of grief.
Led by Caroline Williams' outstanding performance, the entertainingly old-schoolTen Minutes to Midnightdelivers bloody good fun for horror fans.
This Adam Sandler vehicle borrows shamelessly fromIt's A Wonderful LifeandBack To The Future, and fails to produce the necessary laughs that would forgive such imitation.
The Water Horseis a fine family film. It takes a classic tale and infuses it with extra imagination, sly humor, heart, and inventive special effects.
Roberto Benigni misfires wildly with this adaptation of Pinocchio, and the result is an unfunny, poorly-made, creepy vanity project.
Saturated in disquieting mood while leveraging a nightmarishly gonzo performance by Nicolas Cage,Longlegsis a satanic horror that effectively instills panic.
An overblown thriller with formulaic action scenes and poor acting.
The Condemnedis a morally ambiguous, exceedingly violent and mostly forgettable action film.
Bloodsucking Bastardsgets a few gallons of B-movie fun out of its sanguine humor and solid cast, even if it isn't quite as wild as its title might suggest.
Even thoughRoundingdoesn't fully live up to its best expectations, this psychological drama creeps under the skin thanks in part to Namir Smallwood's winning performance.
Built from many of the same ingredients as other Nicholas Sparks tearjerkers,Dear Johnsuffers from its cliched framework, as well as Lasse Hallstrom's curiously detached directing.
Abrahams Boys: A Dracula Storybleeds its source material dry, opting for insipid plotting and misleading story-beats.
Jennifer's Bodyfeatures occasionally clever dialogue, but its horror/comedy premise ultimately fails to be consistently funny or scary enough to satisfy.
Unabashedly campy -- often to its detriment --Swamp Thingis not without its charms, among them Adrienne Barbeau as the damsel in distress.
Smart, well-acted, and suitably chilling,Scare Meuses its familiar horror setting as the backdrop for a fresh deconstruction of standard genre ingredients.
Despite another reliable performance from Don Cheadle,Traitorsuffers from too many cliches and an unfocused narrative.
A smart homage to genre filmmaking,The Similarsis a fun and frightening film that balances socio-political issues with aplomb.
The Clapperis tugged along by the valiant efforts of a talented cast, but it's ultimately not enough to make this wan romantic comedy worth a watch.
Like a colorfully overengineered gewgaw on the shelf,Toysmight look like fun, but its seemingly limitless possibilities lead mainly to confusion and disappointment.
Carmen Ejogo is stranded in a wasteland both figuratively and literally in this disposable thriller, which lacks the cleverness or specificity to give its thin conceit any bite.
Vampire Hunter D's gothic charms may be lost on those unfamiliar with the anime series that spawned it, but the crisp action and nightmarish style will satiate horror aficionados'bloodlust.
Daddy's Headis a chilling and atmospheric slice of psychological horror, delivering unsettling imagery and a tense, unpredictable build-up.
Through the Firemay raise more questions than it answers, but it's no less engrossing for its flaws.
Let the Corpses Tanchallenges the audience's expectations -- and delivers a singularly stylish, unforgettably unique viewing experience in the bargain.
A brawny, entertaining spectacle, but lacking emotional resonance.
Closet Monsteris a unique, understated fable, buoyed by a strong performance from Connor Jessup.
More likely to induce boredom than quicken the pulse,Brahms: The Boy IIis chiefly scary for the way it undermines the effectiveness of its above-average predecessor.
A blunt yet visceral depiction of society's treatment of the elderly,The Amusement Parksees George A. Romero exploring a different -- yet still chilling -- type of terror.
A cheerfully energetic screwball comedy,Only Humanmilks laughs from its culture-clash premise.
Vacancy'srestraint with gore is commendable, the thin characters and B-movie cliches less so.
Donkey Punchbegins as a promising thriller, but loses credibility while adhering too closely to genre conventions.
A smartly ambitious supernatural thriller,Perpetratorblends an assortment of genres and influences into a bloody good time.
A mean-spirited joke without a punchline,Mr. Wrongis so painfully unfunny that Ellen DeGeneres and Bill Pullman's lack of chemistry feels like a total drag despite being the point.
Imaginary's core concept is solid enough to produce a handful of scares, but too much of its potential is lost in a clichéd story that gets bogged down in world-building.
Home is where the heart is inThe Castle, a rousing Australian comedy that gives audiences an endearing family to root for.
Nail-bitingly tense and brilliantly acted,Wait Until Darkis a compact thriller that makes the most of its fiendishly clever premise.
Final Destination 3is more of the same: gory and pointless, with nowhere new to go.
A frightening feature debut from Jacob Chase,Come Playmakes up for its uneven tone by adding real depth to its jarring scares.
The Fog is a so-so remake of a so-so movie, lacking scares, suspense or originality.
The Night Eats the Worldfinds a few unexplored corners in the crowded zombie genre, with a refreshing emphasis on atmosphere and character development.
Child's Playoccasionally stumbles across its tonal tightrope of comedy and horror, but its genuinely creepy monster and some deft direction by Tom Holland makes this chiller stand out on the shelf.
Lacking the hallmarks of his best work,The Wardproves to be a disappointingly mundane swan song for director John Carpenter.
Trespasspits public servants against gangsters in a race for stolen loot -- and thanks to a killer cast and Walter Hill's assured direction, the audience wins.
A hugely influential masterpiece stuffed with iconic sequences,Un Chien Andalouhas lost none of its power to enthrall - or unsettle.

Writer-director Bobcat Goldthwait's first foray into horror doesn't break any new ground, but it does wring fresh terror from a well-worn genre formula -- and offers a few nasty laughs in the bargain.
Boris Karloff's final appearance as the Monster is a fitting farewell before the series descended into self-parody.
Speciesshows flashes of the potential to blend exploitation and sci-fi horror in ingenious ways, but is ultimately mainly interested in flashing star Natasha Henstridge's skin.
Blood Red Skymakes the most of its high-concept vampire story, delivering a fast-paced treat for genre enthusiasts.
As bonkers as a killer arboreal edentate mammal,Slotherhousedelivers B-movie thrills to gore lovers and Greek life haters alike.
The look ofHookis lively indeed but Steven Spielberg directs on autopilot here, giving in too quickly to his sentimental, syrupy qualities.
A kinetic, violent and surprisingly worthy remake of George Romero's horror classic that pays homage to the original while working on its own terms.
It may not offer much they haven't seen before, but strong-stomached exploitation enthusiasts will savorThe Retaliators.
The Covenantplays out like a teen soap opera, full of pretty faces, wooden acting, laughable dialogue, and little suspense.
Bone Tomahawk's peculiar genre blend won't be for everyone, but its gripping performances and a slow-burning story should satisfy those in search of something different.

Slantedblends genre thrills and pointed commentary on racial identity into a daring body horror parable, even if its occasionally unfocused script keeps writer-director Amy Wang's provocative vision from fully landing.
The Last Shiftdoesn't quite reach the potential of its premise, however a powerful performance from Richard Jenkins makes it worth the watch.
Offering a hauntingly lush style and plenty of gore,Bloodthirstysuccessfully captures the nightmarish darkness behind being hungry like the wolf in the quest for fame.
A by-the-numbers slasher that arrived a decade too late, the mostly tediousI Know What You Did Last Summerwill likely only hook diehard fans of the genre.
Lord of Illusionsmay come as something of a disappointment in the context of writer-director Clive Barker's best work, but genre fans should be reasonably diverted.
Murky and too artsy for its own good,Novemberends up being a case of style with little substance.
Smarter, fresher, and funnier than a modern vampire movie has any right to be,What We Do in the Shadowsis bloody good fun.
While it hopes to make viewers swoon over a bond that defies death, all but the most passionate fans of YA romance may struggle to arouse even puppy love forEndless.
Assured work from writer-director Mark O'Brien and Henry Czerny's powerhouse performance putThe Righteousa cut above the average supernatural thriller.
Hypochondriacmistakes a few common horror ailments for exotic afflictions, but this unsettling look at mental illness and trauma proves properly contagious.

Despite a thought-provoking premise and Clint Eastwood's typical flair as director,Hereafterfails to generate much compelling drama, straddling the line between poignant sentimentality and hokey tedium.
Mute Witnessis a slickly crafted horror/thriller with some surprising comic twists.
An eccentric, campy, technically impressive, and frightening picture, James Whale'sBride of Frankensteinhas aged remarkably well.

Deliver Us From Evilis a superb documentary and a searing look at an institution protecting its leaders at the expense of its followers. A profoundly disturbing chronicle of a wolf in sheep's clothing, the film builds a clear-eyed case against pedophile priest Oliver O'Grady, and the Catholic bureaucracy that protected him. The recollections of O'Grady's victims are nothing short of shocking and heartbreaking.
Director Ang Lee revisits the ennui-laden decadence of 1970s suburban America with deft humor and gripping pathos.
An amateurish, unfunny and unscary British horror-comedy that could be accused of misogyny.
The Banishingnever truly taps into its full potential, although this gothic haunted house story still exudes a certain stately chill.
Rent-A-Palsuffers from untapped thematic potential, but Wil Wheaton's unsettling performance makes this unique horror story worth watching.
Well cast and sharply directed,Freshserves as an attention-getting calling card for writer-director Boaz Yakin as well as a gripping urban drama.
Steeped in gallows humor,Stuckis a taut, tense examination of a tragic accident.
A grounded zombie picture with emotional bite,Handling the Undeadshuffles along at a patient pace but succeeds as a poignant and thoughtful addition to the genre.
A remarkable feature debut for director/co-writer Filippo Meneghetti,Two of Ustells a deceptively complex love story while presenting a rich acting showcase for its three leads.
A bleak, but heartbreaking coming-of-age tale that resonates with truth.
Smartly directed by Malgorzata Szumowska,The Other Lambuses a young woman's coming-of-age story to explore the powerful hold cults can have on their followers.
Perplexing, unsettling, and alluring in nearly equal measure,Earwigmay be as difficult to understand as it is to forget.
More unsettling than truly scary,She Willcasts a thoughtful, hypnotic spell made even stronger by its restrained approach and some strong performances.
Perhaps it isn't quite as much fun as a movie about a murderous robot Santa ought to be, but for fans of holiday horror,Christmas Bloody Christmasis still a gift.
Willy's Wonderlandisn't quite as much fun as its premise would suggest -- but it's still got Nicolas Cage beating the hell out of bloodthirsty animatronics, which is nice.
For better or worse,Twin Peaks: Fire Walk With Meis every bit as strange and twisted as you'd expect from David Lynch.
With wit, humor and Guillermo del Toro's fantastic visuals, the entertainingHellboytranscends the derivative nature of the genre.
Though its sweetness borders on sappiness,Roxanneis an unabashedly romantic comedy that remains one of Steve Martin's funniest films.
An uninspired departure for Wes Craven, mired by an uneven premise; beware, this is oneDeadly Friend.
Frightening, funny, and packed with action,Dog Soldiersis well worth checking out for genre fans -- and marks writer-director Neil Marshall as a talent to keep an eye on.
Vacancy'srestraint with gore is commendable, the thin characters and B-movie cliches less so.
This powerful work of essential cinema joins "meta" with "physique," casting Brigite Bardot and director Godard's inspiration Fritz Lang.
Warm Bodiesoffers a sweet, well-acted spin on a genre that all too often lives down to its brain-dead protagonists.

XX's array of distinct female filmmaking voices -- and the empowerment their collection represents -- offer enough thrills to make up for the overall uneven quality that plagues most anthology projects.

Tyler Perry'sThe Family that Preystreads the filmmaker's established terrain of family loyalty and moral uprightness, though the occasionally predictable plot slides forward, aided by an excess of soapy melodrama.
Sissyweaves timely themes into its rich blend of horror and dark humor, topped off by terrific work from a talented cast led by Aisha Dee.
Ben Wheatley'sRebeccaremake is ravishing to behold, but it never quite gets to the heart of the classic source material -- or truly justifies its own existence.
The Lookoutis a genuinely suspenseful and affecting noir due to the great ensemble cast and their complex, realistic characters.
A possession thriller that knows the devil's in the details,Attachmentscares some fresh angles out of a well-worn horror subgenre.
Sam Raimi returns to top form withDrag Me to Hell, a frightening, hilarious, delightfully campy thrill ride.
Night of the Reaperis an atmospheric indie slasher that balances genre enthusiasm and eerie direction to offer a fleeting but engaging diversion for dedicated horror fans.
Less a continuation than an outright reimagining, Sam Raimi transforms his horror tale into a comedy of terrors -- and arguably even improves on the original formula.
A tense, terrifying, and all-around outstanding feature debut for its co-directing duo,The Boy Behind the Doorshould thrill discerning horror fans.
Under the Shadowdeftly blends seemingly disparate genres to deliver an effective chiller with timely themes and thought-provoking social subtext.
Taking an axe to yuletide cheer and everything else in its path,Terrifier 3is a bloody fantastic stocking stuffer for the holiday season.
The rare anthology that maintains a consistently high level of quality,The Mortuary Collectionis a must-see undertaking for horror fans.
Bahman Ghobadi'sHalf Moonis a beautiful and often humorous look into the lives of Kurdish wanderers.
Flickers of frightening promise are muffled by an excessively franchise-focused story inChoose or Die, with frustratingly forgettable results.
Doubling down on gore while largely abandoning the subtext and wit that made the original worthwhile,Candyman: Farewell to the Fleshdisappoints.
A mixed bag of citrus,Bloody Orangesdelivers a basket of scabrous black humor and provocative violence to those who enjoy WTF fare with a French flair.
It's unlikely to leave a lasting impression, butBlack Water: Abyssmay satisfy forgiving genre fans in search of a reasonably effective creature feature.
A possession thriller that knows the devil's in the details,Attachmentscares some fresh angles out of a well-worn horror subgenre.
This tense, paranoid thriller presents Francis Ford Coppola at his finest -- and makes some remarkably advanced arguments about technology's role in society that still resonate today.
With Signs, Shyamalan proves once again an expert at building suspense and giving audiences the chills.
Smart, powerfully acted, and devilishly clever,We Are Still Hereoffers some novel twists on familiar territory -- and heralds the arrival of a major talent in writer-director Ted Geoghegan.
Ghosts of Warblends supernatural horror and period war drama to produce a muddled mashup that proves some ingredients are better left separate.
Excellent performances by Michelle Monaghan and Nathan Fillion and keenly-observed details of small town life elevate this otherwise predictable and inconsistent melodrama.
Blair Witchdoles out a handful of effective scares, but aside from a few new twists, it mainly offers a belated rehash of the original -- and far more memorable -- first film.
A smart re-imagining of fairy tale tropes that's sure to delight children and adults,Enchantedfeatures witty dialogue, sharp animation, and a star turn by Amy Adams.

Train to Busandelivers a thrillingly unique -- and purely entertaining -- take on the zombie genre, with fully realized characters and plenty of social commentary to underscore the bursts of skillfully staged action.
Surreal and visually striking,Taxidermiais, at times, graphic and difficult to watch, but creatively touches on disturbing subjects with imagination and wit.
Grimly stylish, Ken Russell's baroque opus is both provocative and persuasive in its contention that the greatest blasphemy is the leveraging of faith for power.
Roarmay not satisfy in terms of acting, storytelling, or overall production, but the real-life danger onscreen makes it difficult to turn away.

Resident Evil: Retributionoffers everything one might reasonably expect from the fifth installment in a heavily action-dependent franchise -- which means very little beyond stylishly hollow CGI-enhanced set pieces.

Anchored by Daisy Ridley's magnetic performance and a grimly inventive premise,We Bury the Deadkeenly uses familiar zombie tropes as a framework to deliver a beautifully shot, emotionally resonant meditation on loss and grief.
Elevated by Jeremy Moineau's standout performance and Alice Maio Mackay's distinctive direction,Carnage for Christmasunwraps a visually striking, fun whodunnit.
A real polarising movie, this Gallic torture-porn is graphic, brutal, nasty and gruesome and not to everyone's taste.
Despite its stylized and impressive sets, this horror-monster movie mish-mash suffers from endless cliches and wildly implausible plotlines.
Lee Daniels' foray into horror works best when it leans into his melodramatic preoccupations, but it fails to deliver the exorcism goods.
Distasteful and ludicrously plotted,I Know Who Killed Meis a career nadir for all involved -- particularly Lindsay Lohan in a dual role.
Hell Festmight give less demanding horror fans a few decent reasons to scream, but it's neither clever nor frightening enough to leave much of an impression.
Atomospheric but not much else, Cracks is a formless film in search of compelling drama.
DASHCAMis visually and thematically provocative, although the film's grating protagonist undercuts its effectiveness.
Deeply unnerving and surprisingly poignant,The Orphanageis an atmospheric, beautifully crafted haunted house horror film that earns scares with a minimum of blood.
Director Tobe Hooper and a devilishly charismatic James Mason elevate this television adaptation of the Stephen King novel, injecting the vampiric tradition with fresh blood and lingering scares.
It may be based on a true story, but that doesn't excuse the wayThe Possessionrepeatedly falls back on hoary ghost movie clichés -- or the uninentional laughs it provides.
Critics say Bounce is more of a thud. Plot turns feel cliched and contrived, and the romance between Paltrow and Affleck fails to engage.
Spoorstylishly taps into a deep well of anger to deliver an unusual yet largely effective revenge thriller with environmental and feminist overtones.
Grim to a fault,The Golden Gloveembarks on a well-crafted but deeply unsavory descent into the depraved mind and rank brutality of a serial killer.
With miscast stars, a ludicrous plot and an obvious twist, Derailed embodies its name all too aptly.
Dead Ringersserves up a double dose of Jeremy Irons in service of a devilishly unsettling concept and commandingly creepy work from director David Cronenberg.
It won't earn the franchise many new fans, butSaw VIis a surprising step up for what has become an intricately grisly annual tradition.
The tense and well-actedAlways Shinetells an absorbing story while making a sharp, thought-provoking statement on women's roles in Hollywood.
Wes Craven's New Nightmareadds an unexpectedly satisfying - not to mention intelligent - meta layer to a horror franchise that had long since lost its way.
When a Stranger Callsranks among the more misguided remakes in horror history, offering little more than a rote, largely fright-free update to the original.
Dave Made a Mazeoffers decent storytelling but plunges deep into bold stylistic waters, establishing director Bill Watterson as a fresh and inventive filmmaker.
While its humor may strike some as needlessly crude,Unicorn Warsis a visually audacious strike against cultural and military fascism.
Badland Huntersentertains with all-out action, even if the story's far less impressive than the fight scenes.
Narratively challenging and visually haunting,We're All Going to the World's Fairadds a uniquely ambitious and unsettling entry to the crowded coming-of-age genre.
Insidious: The Last Keyoffers franchise star Lin Shaye another welcome opportunity to take the lead, but her efforts aren't enough to rescue this uninspired sequel.
Though Streep is dependably terrific in her role, the rest of the movie is too sitcom-ish, and the romance itself is dull.
Stephen Lang remains a thrillingly imposing presence inDon't Breathe 2, but this sequel's story strains to find a sensible way forward for his character.
A twisted and bloody spoof on office life,Severancenicely balances comedy and nasty horror.
A peculiar breach of morality that leads with pretension: Denis Villeneuve guidesMaelstromthrough teetering absurdity without malicious intent.
I Spit on Your Graveis as aggressively exploitative as its title suggests, although as a crude rejoinder to misogyny, it packs a certain amount of undeniable power.
It isn't Tim Burton's best work, butSleepy Hollowentertains with its stunning visuals and creepy atmosphere.
Identity Thief's few laughs are attributable to Melissa McCarthy and Jason Bateman, who labor mightily to create a framework for the movie's undisciplined plotline.
They're back, but this hollow sequel retains none of the charm or suspense that made the originalPoltergeistsuch a haunting specter.
While occasionally funny and relentlessly gory,Girls With Ballsis neither particularly strong on cheap thrills nor a very remarkable entry to the trash horror genre.
A smooth blend of scares and dark humor,Satanic Hispanicsis a well-crafted horror anthology whose occasionally jarring tonal shifts are easy to forgive.
Sawensnares audiences with a deceptively clever plot and a myriad of memorable, nasty set pieces, but its lofty ambitions are undercut by a nihilistic streak that feels more mean than profound.
An uneasy blend of action and comedy,Date Nightdoesn't quite live up to the talents of its two leads, but Steve Carell and Tina Fey still manage to shine through most of the movie's flaws.
A landmark in animation (and a huge influence on the medium of music video), Disney'sFantasiais a relentlessly inventive blend of the classics with phantasmagorical images.
Hanks' performance in the lead role is inspired, but this is a relatively minor offering from the Coen brothers.
Visually polished but narratively derivative and overall muddled,Muteis a would-be sci-fi epic whose title serves as an unfortunate guide to how it might be best enjoyed.

Featuring state-of-the-art special effects, terrific performances, and a majestic sense of spectacle, Peter Jackson's remake ofKing Kongis a potent epic that's faithful to the spirit of the 1933 original.
The Strangershas a handful of genuinely scary moments, but they're not enough to elevate the end results above standard slasher fare.
Some surprising touches and a generally thoughtful approach help elevateSignificant Otherabove standard wilderness survival stories.
Home is where the heart is inThe Castle, a rousing Australian comedy that gives audiences an endearing family to root for.
Chiefly of interest to Nicolas Cage completists and hardcore B-movie fans, this action thriller suffers from an unfortunate lack ofPrimalenergy.
A maudlin misfire,Father's Daymanages the difficult task of making Billy Crystal and Robin Williams woefully unfunny.
Friday the 13th Part 2sets the template for the franchise to follow with more teen victims, more gruesome set pieces, and fewer reasons to keep following along.
Dracula's Daughterextends the Universal horror myth in an interesting direction, but the talky script and mild atmosphere undermine its ambition.

Jeffrey Combs' Herbert West remains a wickedly devious presence, butBride of Re-Animatorcan't revive the original's anarchically inventive spark, making it a fitfully entertaining but greatly diminished sequel.
A frustratingly uneven all-star disaster drama,Outbreakultimately proves only mildly contagious and leaves few lasting side effects.
Though it sacrifices some depth in its characterizations,From Beyondstands as a stunningly grotesque Lovecraft adaptation with a dazzling blend of chilling effects and brainy, cosmic horror.
Lacking any of the comedic bite that audiences have come to expect from a Mel Brooks farce, this vampire parody just plain ol' sucks.
This Adam Sandler vehicle borrows shamelessly fromIt's A Wonderful LifeandBack To The Future, and fails to produce the necessary laughs that would forgive such imitation.
Rachel Weisz puts on a compelling smoldering act though the film suffers from a literal-minded approach to the material.
While it might prove somewhat satisfying for devout sci-fi fans,Pandorum's bloated, derivative plot ultimately leaves it drifting in space.
Primevalis a low-quality horror film, which due to the inane political messages does not even qualify as campy fun.
Well-acted in the execution of its provocative "what-if?" premise.
It strains to up the gore and self-awareness of it predecessor, and -- despite some game celebrity cameos -- the result is a dispiriting echo of 2010's horror-comedy.
This dark psycho-thriller from Jennifer Lynch, is violent, sharp and baffling, but not to everyone's taste.
Guillermo del Toro's unique feature debut is not only gory and stylish, but also charming and intelligent.
Visually audacious, disorienting, and just plain weird,Videodrome's musings on technology, entertainment, and politics still feel fresh today.
The Hole in the Groundartfully exploits parental fears with a well-made horror outing that makes up in sheer effectiveness what it lacks in originality.
Led by Rebecca Hall's gripping central performance,The Night Houseoffers atmospheric horror that engages intellectually as well as emotionally.
Tape's stagebound feel is balanced by the engrossing psychodrama of its storyline, which allows a committed cast to shine.
The Water Horseis a fine family film. It takes a classic tale and infuses it with extra imagination, sly humor, heart, and inventive special effects.
Amenloses some of its impact to a protracted running time and deliberate pace, but Ulrich Tukur's powerful performance helps bring the story's worthy themes to life.
A real polarising movie, this Gallic torture-porn is graphic, brutal, nasty and gruesome and not to everyone's taste.
Oliver!transforms Charles Dickens' muckraking novel into a jaunty musical Victorian fairytale, buoyed by Ron Moody's charming star turn and Onna White's rapturous choreography.
Thrilling and relentless from start to finish, Yeon Sang-ho'sSeoul Stationis a layered and vicious entry into the zombie genre.
Death Wishis undeniably exploitation fare -- and also undeniably effective.
Led by a daring performance from Jean Dujardin,Deerskinfinds writer-director Quentin Dupieux working in a more accessible -- yet still distinctive -- vein.
Featuring dazzling, disorienting cinematography from the great James Wong Howe and a strong lead performance by Rock Hudson, Seconds is a compellingly paranoid take on the legend of Faust.
Nicole Kidman's coiled intensity and muscular direction by Phillip Noyce give this nautical thriller a disquieting sense of dread.
Zach Cregger spins an expertly crafted yarn of terrifying mystery and thrilling intrigue inWeapons, a sophomore triumph that solidifies his status as a master of horror.
Strong direction and an arresting sense of visual style make the vampire love triangleClimate of the Huntera balmy treat for fans of off-kilter horror.
Smart, solidly crafted, and palpably tense,10 Cloverfield Lanemakes the most of its confined setting and outstanding cast -- and suggests a new frontier for franchise filmmaking.
Boasting top-notch special effects and exuberant direction from Peter Jackson,The Frightenersis visually striking but tonally uneven.
Insidious: Chapter 2is decidedly short on the tension and surprises that made its predecessor so chilling.
Elements of Scream reappear in a vastly inferior vehicle.
It isn't Tim Burton's best work, butSleepy Hollowentertains with its stunning visuals and creepy atmosphere.
The Strangers: Prey at Nightmay appeal to fans of the original who've been jonesing for a sequel, but its thin story and ironic embrace of genre tropes add up to a bloody step back.
Delightfully dark and impressively ambitious,A Wounded Fawnoffers a grimly distinctive treat for slasher fans.
Baskincomplements its gory thrills with heavy atmosphere and deliberate pacing, adding up to a horror outing that plays with the mind as enthusiastically as it ruins the appetite.
Offering a hauntingly lush style and plenty of gore,Bloodthirstysuccessfully captures the nightmarish darkness behind being hungry like the wolf in the quest for fame.
Murky and too artsy for its own good,Novemberends up being a case of style with little substance.

One of the silent era's most influential masterpieces,Nosferatu's eerie, gothic feel -- and a chilling performance from Max Schreck as the vampire -- set the template for the horror films that followed.

Pet Sematary: Bloodlinestakes half-hearted stabs at expanding the world of Stephen King's original story, but they're largely lost in a substandard story that mistakes bland gore for effective horror.
The Clapperis tugged along by the valiant efforts of a talented cast, but it's ultimately not enough to make this wan romantic comedy worth a watch.
A Cure for Wellnessboasts a surfeit of visual style, but it's wasted on a derivative and predictable story whose twists, turns, and frights have all been more effectively dealt before.
Half social allegory, half home-invasion thriller,The Purgeattempts to make an intelligent point, but ultimately devolves into numbing violence and tired clichés.
A Svankmajer movie is not for everyone, but he displays his usual creative flair for surreal imagery.
GiveSeed of Chuckycredit for embracing the increasing absurdity of the franchise -- even if the end results really aren't all that funny or entertaining.
While a marginally fascinating look at a taboo subject,Zoois bogged down by its overly artistic presentation.
A well-acted film noir with arresting visuals.
47 Meters Down: Uncagedmay not be as ruthlessly efficient as its finned villains, but fans of shark peril thrillers should find it just chummy enough.
The Moogaiattempts to creep its way into the annals of horror history but falls flat under the weight of its poor execution.
Gwyneth Paltrow and Anthony Hopkins give exceptional performances in a film that intelligently tackles the territory between madness and genius.
Competently made, but everything is a bit too familiar.
Spiral: From the Book of Sawsuggests an interesting new direction for theSawfranchise, even if the gory sum is rather less than its parts.
The Pale Blue Eyelacks its source material's piercing gaze, but this well-cast mystery is just intriguing enough to investigate.
This portrait of a groundbreaking photographer lacks the daring of its subject.
Perplexing, unsettling, and alluring in nearly equal measure,Earwigmay be as difficult to understand as it is to forget.
A slasher with flair and crafty patience for the kill,The Stylistmarks writer-director Jill Gevargizian as an uncommonly sharp genre filmmaker.
Marrying environmental themes with bloody thrills, this Gallic entry into the shark attack canon ultimately lands on the right side of ridiculous fun.
Kinetically directed by Danny Boyle,28 Days Lateris both a terrifying zombie movie and a sharp political allegory.
Despite a panel of X-Files' alums at the helm and a promising premise, flighty performances and poor execution keepFinal Destinationfrom ever taking off.
Pride and Prejudice and Zombiesmanages to wring a few fun moments out of its premise, but never delivers the thoroughly kooky mashup its title suggests.
A deep-sea thriller with an unusually strong cast and potent ideas,Leviathanquickly plunges into an abyss of weak thrills and lame kills.
Challenging and rewarding in equal measure,Climaxcaptures writer-director Gaspar Noé working near his technically brilliant and visually distinctive peak.

Newcomer Saskia Rosendahl gives an astonishingly assured performance in the title role, and director Cate Shortland establishes a delicate, disturbingly ominous tone in the powerful World War II dramaLore.
Even if the somewhat convoluted plot falls apart upon close inspection,Dead Againproves Kenneth Branagh has a solid knack for enjoyable pulp.
Gray Matterstries emulating Hollywood screwball classics, but the rapid fire dialogue and witticisms comes off only as a contrived gimmick.
Hitchcock proves he can wring suspense from the most confined of settings aboard a raft teeming with vivid personalities in this maritime thriller.
Elevated by writer-director Clive Barker's fiendishly unique vision,Hellraiseroffers a disquieting - and sadistically smart - alternative to mindless gore.
Skinamarinkcan be more confounding than frightening, but for viewers able or willing to dial into its unique wavelength, this unsettling film will be difficult to shake.
The Pestaims for zany comedy with social commentary, misses, and lands squarely at offensive misfire thanks to John Leguizamo's over-caffeinated performance and a script laden with stereotypes.
Terrifier 2outdoes the original in every way -- which makes it bad news for the squeamish, but a bloody good time for genre enthusiasts.
Similar to the original in all the right ways -- but with enough changes to stand on its own --Let Me Inis the rare Hollywood remake that doesn't add insult to inspiration.
A nerve-jangling thriller that exposes how reactionary extremism can lead to a road of destruction,Sovereignis ruled by Nick Offerman's unforgettably unhinged performance.
A wickedly delightful genre mashup led by Melissa Barrera,Your Monsteris pleasantly packed with songs, guts, and heart.
Paranormal Activity: The Ghost Dimensionties up some of the franchise's lingering questions, but six films into the series, the thrills are mostly gone.
Kids vs. Aliensoccasionally comes close to offering the goofy fun promised by the title, although the end result is ultimately rather forgettable.
The Green Infernomay not win writer-director Eli Roth many new converts, but fans of his flair for gory spectacle should find it a suitably gruesome diversion.
Pet Semataryis a bruising horror flick that wears its quirks on its sleeves, to the detriment of its scare factor.
Vulgar more than lives up to its title.
Eraser's shoot-'em-up action might show off some cutting edge weaponry, but its rote story is embarrassingly obsolete.
Well-acted by its young leads,Martyrs Lanetells a slow-burning ghost story that gathers real emotional weight.
Im Sang-soo's remake ofThe Housemaidstruggles to escape the shadow of the original, but offers its own unique -- and decidedly sensual -- pleasures.
There are good things and there areBad Things, and while the film may not rise above its potential -- or source of inspiration -- it delivers a memorable experience.
Henry: Portrait of a Serial Killeris an effective, chilling profile of a killer that is sure to shock and disturb.
Adding dashes of horror to a noir premise,Midnightersis a genre-bending chiller that delights as often as it scares.
We Have a Ghosthas a fun concept and a talented cast; unfortunately, this spookily uneven '80s pastiche also has very little idea of what to do with them.
Hair Highisn't first-tier Plympton, but like the rest of the animator's work, this is an assuredly odd tale that should resonate with fans of strange cinema.
A remarkable achievement,Playtime's packs every scene with sight gags and characters that both celebrates and satirizes the urbanization of modern life.
Despite typically strong work from Maggie Q,Fear the Nightis a fairly generic and disappointingly tension-free thriller from writer-director Neil LaBute.
It's hard not to admire its often ungainly attempts to achieve its ambitions, butThe Scary of Sixty-Firstis a horror provocation that disappoints as often as it thrills.
Similar to the original in all the right ways -- but with enough changes to stand on its own --Let Me Inis the rare Hollywood remake that doesn't add insult to inspiration.
Tape's stagebound feel is balanced by the engrossing psychodrama of its storyline, which allows a committed cast to shine.
The FPS sections are sure to please fans of the video game, but lacking in plot and originality to please other moviegoers.

Prisoners of the Ghostlandis far from Sono's most distinctive work, but viewers in the mood for a deliriously gonzo genre mash-up featuring an explosive performance from Nicolas Cage just might have a ball.
A cheesy monster B-movie.
Scary Movieoverloads on crudity and grossness to get its laughs.

A direct continuation of28 Years Laterthat ups the gore while deepening the dread,The Bone Templeis finely adorned by Nia DaCosta's unnerving direction as well as Ralph Fiennes and Jack O'Connell's inspired performances.

Without the gothic melodrama and berserk wit of its predecessor,Darkman II: The Return of Durantstruggles to rise above its made-for-video roots despite flashes of fun and admirable cinematic ambition.
Bombastic and intentionally gross,Repo! The Genetic Operahas a unique style but lacks the wit and substance to be involving.
Its puerile premise invites low expectations, but viewers who sit down in search of boldly humorous provocations won't leaveButt Boybrokenhearted.
Thanks in large part to Anthony Hopkins' layered performance,Magicis an unusual albeit creepily effective experience.
A gothic, campy, raunchy comedy elevated by Cassandra Peterson's iconic persona yet driven off course by one-note jokes, this isn't the best — or worst — introduction toElvira, Mistress of the Dark.
Smart, dynamic, and fast-paced,Saloummixes tones and genres into a tart, smoothly blended treat.
George A. Romero's debut set the template for the zombie film, and features tight editing, realistic gore, and a sly political undercurrent.
An amateurish, unfunny and unscary British horror-comedy that could be accused of misogyny.
Powerfully acted and sensitively directed,Babyteethoffers audiences a coming-of-age story that's messier -- and more rewarding -- than most.
Comedy is too hit-or-miss in this slasher spoof.
Chiefly of interest to Nicolas Cage completists and hardcore B-movie fans, this action thriller suffers from an unfortunate lack ofPrimalenergy.
Michael Keaton's devious poltergeist still has plenty of juice left in this madcap return to form for Tim Burton, marrying charming practical effects and ghoulish gags to provide a fun fun time.
An amusingly perceptive comedy about the push and pull of friendship,Sacramentoglows with charm even when its characters drive each other mad.
Winnie-the-Pooh: Blood and Honey 2represents an improvement over the original in most respects, although the Poohniverse remains a place made for hardcore slasher fans.
An inventive horror flick with provocative ideas about fidelity to go along with its unnerving frights,Honey Bunchremains thrillingly unpredictable to the very end.
Taking a hammer and chisel to a quintessential fairy tale,The Ugly Stepsister's masterful application of gore and subversion are the stuff that nightmares are made of.
A slimy, B-movie homage oozing with affection for low-budget horror films,Slitheris creepy and funny -- if you've got the stomach for it.
Audaciously gross, wickedly clever, and possibly Demi Moore's finest hour,The Substanceis a gasp-inducing feat from writer-director Coralie Fargeat.
A scarily effective horror outing,Veronicaproves it doesn't take fancy or exotic ingredients to craft skin-crawling genre thrills.
Mike Nichols wrangles agreeably amusing performances from Robin Williams and Nathan Lane in this fun, if not quite essential, remake of the French comedyLa Cage aux Folles.
John and the Hole's enticingly unique premise and disquieting atmosphere are often at odds with a meandering story that prevents this thriller from fulfilling its creepy potential.
Whether you choose to see it as a statement on consumer culture or simply a special effects-heavy popcorn flick,Gremlinsis a minor classic.
Child's Playoccasionally stumbles across its tonal tightrope of comedy and horror, but its genuinely creepy monster and some deft direction by Tom Holland makes this chiller stand out on the shelf.
The Possession of Hannah Gracefeints at real horror just often enough to offer hints of the movie it should have been -- and further frustrate viewers seeking a good scare.
The movie looks good and has its moments. But, overall, the movie borders on boredom.
Heavy with symbolism and deliberately paced,Orpheusmay not be for everyone -- but as an example of Jean Cocteau's eccentric genius, it's all but impossible not to recommend.
One of Zhang's smaller films, Happy Times is nevertheless moving and bittersweet.
A frightening feature debut from Jacob Chase,Come Playmakes up for its uneven tone by adding real depth to its jarring scares.
Dare's sharp writing and appealing young cast are often enough to outweigh its jarringly jumbled narrative.
A boring, suspense-freeParanormal Activityrip-off that feels long even at just 90 minutes.
Conceptually strong but uneven in execution,There's Something Wrong with the Childrenis a serviceable horror movie that could have been much more.
Playing exactly to expectations for a movie about killer fish run amok,Piranha 3-Ddishes out gore, guffaws and gratuitous nudity with equal glee.
It's far from the most toothless shark thriller, butGreat Whitelacks the killer storytelling instinct necessary to stay afloat.
Tape's stagebound feel is balanced by the engrossing psychodrama of its storyline, which allows a committed cast to shine.
St. Agathamakes admittedly uneven use of its story's well-worn religious tropes, but the overall effect may still be just chilling enough for horror fans.
A squirm-inducing period piece that locates true horror in both mind and spirit,The Devil's Bathmight be Severin Fiala and Veronika Franz's most chilling directorial effort yet.
Featuring Robert Mitchum's formidable performance as a child-hunting preacher,The Night of the Hunteris a disturbing look at good and evil.
The Strangershas a handful of genuinely scary moments, but they're not enough to elevate the end results above standard slasher fare.
Funny, scary, and thought-provoking,Get Outseamlessly weaves its trenchant social critiques into a brilliantly effective and entertaining horror/comedy thrill ride.
Anchored by a pair of terrific performances swathed in vivid colors, Spanish auteur Pedro Almodóvar's English-language feature debut attests to his universal fluency in provocative filmmaking.
Despite an interesting premise and spooky atmospherics, Chernobyl Diaries is mostly short on suspense and originality.
A brutal, relentlessly grimy shocker with taut performances, slick gore effects, and a haunting finale.
Smart, well-acted, and suitably chilling,Scare Meuses its familiar horror setting as the backdrop for a fresh deconstruction of standard genre ingredients.
While it hopes to make viewers swoon over a bond that defies death, all but the most passionate fans of YA romance may struggle to arouse even puppy love forEndless.
A rich visual treat for film fans,Mad Godproves that even in the age of CGI, the cinematic allure of stop-motion animation remains strong.
BloodRayneis an absurd sword-and-sorcery vid-game adaptation from schlock-maestro Uwe Boll, featuring a distinguished (and slumming) cast.
Funny, scary, and thought-provoking,Get Outseamlessly weaves its trenchant social critiques into a brilliantly effective and entertaining horror/comedy thrill ride.
A brilliantly unsettling blend of body horror and psychological thriller,Saint Maudmarks an impressive debut for writer-director Rose Glass.
Led by Ezra Dewey's standout performance,The Djinnserves up a scary, sleekly effective cautionary tale about being careful what you wish for.
As lumberingly single-minded as its homicidal star,Friday the 13th - The Final Chapteradds another rote entry to an increasingly labored franchise.
